---
title: "HOW-TO: Ubuntu Karmic 9.10 on HP TouchSmart tx2-1050el Notebook PC"
date: 2009-10-29
forum: Hardware
---

### Post by nicolaasuni on 2009-10-29
**This is an HOW-TO guide for solving some problems related to the installation of Ubuntu 9.10 (Karmic Koala) AMD64 Desktop on [HP TouchSmart tx2-1050el Notebook PC]("http://h10025.www1.hp.com/ewfrf/wc/product?lc=en&dlc=en&cc=us&lang=en&product=3878230") (*[B]Product Number **NJ433EA*).[/B]

The Ubuntu version I'm using is a daily snapshot (2009-10-27).
```
2.6.31-14-generic #48-Ubuntu SMP Fri Oct 16 14:05:01 UTC 2009 x86_64 GNU/Linux
```
(now I'm working with  2.6.31-19-generic)

Copy the ISO of "Ubuntu 9.10 (Karmic Koala) AMD64 Desktop CD" to a bootable USB pendrive using usb-creator and reserve some space to store your data.


Insert the USB pendrive on the notebook and power it on.

Push the ESC button on the keyboard to select the boot drive (choose the USB pendrive).

Launch Ubuntu in Live mode (first option).

Due to a [bug]("https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/ubiquity/+bug/432422"), you have to make the following changes to speedup the installtion process:

Open the terminal (*Applications > Accessories > Terminal*) to edit a file:
```

sudo gedit /usr/lib/ubiquity/user-setup/user-setup-apply

```chage the following line ```
dd if=/dev/zero of=$device 2>/dev/null || true
``` to
 ```
dd bs=16m if=/dev/zero of=$device 2>/dev/null || true
``` The original command was reading 512 bytes of zeroes, writing 512 bytes of zeroes to the disk, and looping. Very slow -- one sector at a time. Adding "bs=16m" means that each write zeroes 16 megabytes of disk. It's about 1000x as fast.

Now you can install Ubuntu as normal, following the indications.

Once installed reboot the system and log in.

Update the sytem using the following commands:
```

sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get dist-upgrade

```Restart the system if required.

Go to *System > Administration > Hardware Drivers* and install the following drivers:

[LIST]
[*]Broadcom STA wireless driver
[*]Software modem
[*]ATI/AMD proprietary FGLRX graphics driver
[/LIST]
Restart the system.

Edit the following file to enable audio and mic:
```
sudo gedit /etc/modprobe.d/alsa-base.conf
```add the following line at the end
```
options snd-hda-intel model=acer-dmic
```Due to a bug that stops sound working on boot and hibernate/suspend, I force alsa reload by modifying the following scripts:
```
sudo gedit /etc/rc.local
```and add the following line before the exit:
```
/sbin/alsa force-reload
```Create a script to reload alsa on hibernate/suspend:
```
sudo gedit /etc/pm/sleep.d/fixsound
``````

#!/bin/bash
alsa force-reload

```make the script executable
```
sudo chmod +x /etc/pm/sleep.d/fixsound
```To enable the N-trig digitizer (touch-screen) you need a patched hid-ntrig.c that will be included in kernel 2.6.32 ([see the HOW TO: Set up the HP TX2z and Dell XT & XT2 (N-trig digitizer) in Ubuntu]("http://ubuntu-ky.ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1252492"))[B].
[/B]
You have to remove the following package:
```
sudo apt-get purge wacom-tools xserver-xorg-input-wacom
```**Follow the instructions at [How to Add Pen and Touch Patch to Karmic]("http://linuxfans.keryxproject.org/?page_id=23") to patch the N-Trig kernel module in Karmic.**

edit the following file:
```
sudo gedit /etc/modules
```and add the following line at the end
```
hp-wmi
```Replace your xorg.conf with the following to enable the N-trig digitizer touchscreen:

```
sudo gedit /etc/X11/xorg.conf
``````

Section "Screen"
    Identifier    "Default Screen"
    DefaultDepth    24
EndSection

Section "Module"
    Load    "glx"
EndSection

Section "Device"
    Identifier    "Default Device"
    Driver    "fglrx"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice" 
    Identifier        "stylus" 
    Driver            "wacom" 
    Option        "Device"    "/dev/input/by-path/pci-0000:00:14.5-usb-0:2:1.0-event-mouse"
    Option            "Type"        "stylus"
    Option            "USB"         "on" 
    Option            "Button2"     "3"  # make side-switch a right button 
    Option        "TopX"        "0"
    Option        "TopY"        "0"
    Option        "BottomX"    "9600"
    Option        "BottomY"    "7200"
EndSection 

#Section "InputDevice" 
#      Identifier        "eraser" 
#      Driver            "wacom" 
#    Option        "Device"    "/dev/input/wacom"
#      Option            "Type"           "eraser"
#      Option            "USB"            "on" 
#EndSection 

Section "InputDevice" 
    Identifier        "touch" 
    Driver            "wacom" 
    Option        "Device"    "/dev/input/by-path/pci-0000:00:14.5-usb-0:2:1.0-event-mouse"
    Option          "Type"           "touch"
    Option          "USB"            "on" 
    Option        "TopX"        "0"
    Option        "TopY"        "0"
    Option        "BottomX"    "9600"
    Option        "BottomY"    "7200"
EndSection

Section "ServerLayout"
    Identifier    "Default Layout"
    Screen        "Default Screen"
    InputDevice    "stylus"    "SendCoreEvents"
#   Remove the comment below if you have an eraser.
#    InputDevice    "eraser"    "SendCoreEvents"
    InputDevice    "touch"        "SendCoreEvents"
EndSection

```To automatically rotate the screen when you rotate the display, use create the following script on your home folder:

```
gedit ~/autorotate.sh
``````

#!/bin/sh
OLDMODE=$(cat /sys/devices/platform/hp-wmi/tablet)
while true; do
    MODE=$(cat /sys/devices/platform/hp-wmi/tablet)
    if [ "$MODE" != "$OLDMODE" ]
    then
        #echo "$MODE - $OLDMODE"
        case "$MODE" in
            "0")
                # Do something
                echo "Normal mode"
                xrandr -o normal 
                xsetwacom set stylus rotate NONE 
                xsetwacom set touch rotate NONE 
                #xsetwacom set eraser rotate NONE
                #cellwriter --hide-window
                ;;
            "1")
                # Do something else
                echo "Tablet mode"
                xrandr -o inverted 
                xsetwacom set stylus rotate HALF 
                xsetwacom set touch rotate HALF 
                #xsetwacom set eraser rotate HALF 
                #cellwriter --show-window
                ;;
        esac
        OLDMODE=$MODE
    fi
    sleep 2s
done

```make the script executable
```
chmod +x ~/autorotate.sh
```Go to *System > Preferences > Startup Applications* and Add the above script.


Create a script to rotate the screen 90 degrees a time:

```
gedit ~/rotate.sh
``````

#!/bin/sh 

# Find the line in "xrandr -q --verbose" output that contains current screen orientation and "strip" out current orientation. 

rotation="$(xrandr -q --verbose | grep 'connected' | egrep -o  '\) (normal|left|inverted|right) \(' | egrep -o '(normal|left|inverted|right)')" 

# Using current screen orientation proceed to rotate screen and input tools. 

case "$rotation" in 
    normal) 
#    -rotate to the left 
    xrandr -o left 
    xsetwacom set stylus rotate CCW 
    xsetwacom set touch rotate CCW 
    xsetwacom set eraser rotate CCW 
    ;; 
    left) 
#    -rotate to inverted 
    xrandr -o inverted 
    xsetwacom set stylus rotate HALF 
    xsetwacom set touch rotate HALF 
    xsetwacom set eraser rotate HALF 
    ;; 
    inverted) 
#    -rotate to the right 
    xrandr -o right 
    xsetwacom set stylus rotate  CW 
    xsetwacom set touch rotate CW 
    xsetwacom set eraser rotate CW  
    ;; 
    right) 
#    -rotate to normal 
    xrandr -o normal 
    xsetwacom set stylus rotate NONE 
    xsetwacom set touch rotate NONE 
    xsetwacom set eraser rotate NONE 
    ;; 
esac

```make the script executable
```
chmod +x ~/rotate.sh
```Now you can associate the script to a button or make a launcher with an icon.
Open System > Preferences > Keyboard Shortcuts and associate the rotate.sh script to the "gesture" button on the display (the button in the middle).

Enable the following repository on *System > Administration > Software Sources > Other Software* :
[COLOR=Navy]http://archive.canonical.com/ubuntu karmic partner[/COLOR]

Install acroread using *System > Administration > Synaptic Package Manager*

Use the following command to install firefox plugin:
```
sudo sh /opt/Adobe/Reader9/Browser/install_browser_plugin
```Once installed use the following command to disable digital signature support that chrashes Acrobat Reader when a signed document is opened:
```
sudo mv /opt/Adobe/Reader9/Reader/intellinux/plug_ins/DigSig.api /opt/Adobe/Reader9/Reader/intellinux/plug_ins/DigSig.api.disable
```

### Post by nema.arpit on 2009-10-30
Markku

Try the following :
Restore the original **hid-ntrig.ko** and **hid-wacom.ko** ( in attached tar, in case you did not back 'em up)
purge the packages wacom-tools and xserver-xorg-input-wacom.

**Patch, compile** and **install** the linuxwacom drivers : from thread [http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=6546012]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=6546012#post6546012")
[B]
1)[/B]Download the official linuxwacom driver from [http://linuxwacom.sourceforge.net/](http://linuxwacom.sourceforge.net/)
```
cd ./Desktop

wget http://nchc.dl.sourceforge.net/project/linuxwacom/linuxwacom/0.8.4-4/linuxwacom-0.8.4-4.tar.bz2

```**In case wget does not work, check the site and download the latest version**.Also download the n-trig patch in the attachment and rename it to n-trig.patch.Make sure the tar and n-trig.patch are in same directory.
**2)** Install needed libraries
```

sudo apt-get update

sudo apt-get install build-essential libx11-dev libxi-dev x11proto-input-dev xserver-xorg-dev tk8.4-dev tcl8.4-dev libncurses5-dev

sudo apt-get upgrade

sudo apt-get install wacom-tools xserver-xorg-input-wacom

sudo apt-get purge wacom-tools xserver-xorg-input-wacom

```**3)**Extract and patch the linuxwacom (**important** )
```

tar xjvf linuxwacom-0.8.4-3.tar.bz2
patch linuxwacom-0.8.4-3/src/xdrv/wcmUSB.c < n-trig.patch
```[B]
4)[/B]
The patched linuxwacom won't make without hid-ids .  So get it and put it in place

```
wget http://kernel.ubuntu.com/git-repos/ubuntu/linux-2.6/drivers/hid/hid-ids.h

sudo cp ./hid-ids.h /lib/modules/`uname -r`/build/drivers/hid/hid-ids.h

```**5)**Now make and install
```

cd linuxwacom-0.8.4-3
./configure --enable-wacom --prefix=/usr
make
sudo make install

```[B]
6)[/B]Now update you xorg.conf (nicolaasuni's xorg works ;however don't just replace your xorg ([SIZE=1]you might be using different graphics driver[/SIZE]) ,rather add the relevant sections), and **importantly** add wacom to /etc/modules
```

sudo gedit /etc/modules

```Also add hp-wmi to get auto-rotation working (see nicolaasuni's post above)
Reboot and see if this works ( did for me -the steps are same as those which I followed to set up touch on Arch)

_**UPDATE**_ : I replaced the hid-*.ko files with the ones nicolaasuni provided and now the Touch is much better than before . I would suggest to everyone to try it : just make sure you backup you original driver files first.

### Post by Ayuthia on 2009-10-31
> **RNA-Wrangler said:**
> Interesting. Just a few novice questions though:
[LIST]
[*]When you add wacom to /etc/modules, is it just 'wacom', or hp-wmi?
[*]Presuming that the stylus and touch is calibrated properly (btw, is there anyway to do that manually, or by GUI?), is the button working and does the cursor still face the wrong way upon rotation?
[*]Oh, and to anyone reading, do the firmware considerations only apply to the original Windows OS (as in, if it had Windows Vista first, then installed W7, is it still classed as having Windows Vista firmware)?
[*]Finally, does the firmware exist independent of the OS...Will it still be there if I wipe Vista? (getting touch to work perfectly on W7 and Ubuntu is the only reason my copy of Vista is still floundering within my HDD)
[/LIST]

I'll do a couple of tests (I lost sound after changing the hid as per this guide...strange thing is, sound worked perfectly for ~30 boots previously, so there was no need for the sound fix). I wonder if anyone- *at all*- has managed to get the cursor the right way up on rotate...

> ]When you add wacom to /etc/modules, is it just 'wacom', or hp-wmi?
The wacom module it is just 'wacom'.  The hp-wmi is for the rotation.  

> Presuming that the stylus and touch is calibrated properly (btw, is there anyway to do that manually, or by GUI?), is the button working and does the cursor still face the wrong way upon rotation?
You can try wacomcpl (it is a GUI app but there is no menu item so you have to call it from the Terminal) to help calibrate your stylus.  I am not for sure if you will get any options for the touch.  As for the button, which button are you talking about?  And for the cursor, it should face the right way after the rotation.

> Oh, and to anyone reading, do the firmware considerations only apply to the original Windows OS (as in, if it had Windows Vista first, then installed W7, is it still classed as having Windows Vista firmware)?If you install the N-Trig drivers for Windows 7, it will have the Windows 7 firmware.  But as a warning, the newly released N-Trig firmware for Windows 7 on HP will prevent the touch from working.  They are now reporting the finger data differently so the Wacom driver will not be able to handle it.  The RC version and the Vista firmware will still work with the Wacom driver.

> Finally, does the firmware exist independent of the OS...Will it still be there if I wipe Vista? (getting touch to work perfectly on W7 and Ubuntu is the only reason my copy of Vista is still floundering within my HDD)Once the firmware is installed, you don't really need to have the OS there anymore.  However, sometimes the firmware will lose contact with the device and might need to be reset.  I have only been able to fix it through Windows.

### Post by Ayuthia on 2009-10-31
> **RNA-Wrangler said:**
> A shame this doesn't work on live-cd's...or does it?;)

I suppose you could apply the same changes to the RAM file system that the live-cd sets up, just so long as you're really careful with the restart button (X-server restart is your friend under these circumstances).

There is a chance that it will work out of the box on the Lucid Lynx release provided that you don't use the N-Trig Windows 7 final release firmware.  The N-Trig patch has been submitted again into linuxwacom, but I don't know whether it will be accepted.  The final kernel patch that Rafi Rubin created for the N-Trig device is in 2.6.32 so it will show up in Lynx.

### Post by nema.arpit on 2009-11-01
@RNA-Wrangler 
The stylus probably "responded" in jaunty because it was using .fdi method.
In fresh Karmic there is no entry in 10-wacom.fdi about n-trig (I think - currently I have no 10-wacom.fdi :p)
**Xorg **You can use nicolaasuni's xorg provided you have the proprietary Ati drivers enabled. Just adding the the relevant sections to xorg.conf is sufficient though (i.e **ServerLayout** and **InputDevice** for stylus as well as touch.)
**Patching **I don't know about the warning messages during patching : I never got any.Post them here and maybe someone can figure something out.
 **Location of files** : at the very least the linuxwacom-tar extract and the n-trig.patch should be in the same folder,which should be your current directory in terminal.Also before you run 
```
./configure --enable-wacom --prefix=/usr 
``` change directory to linuxwacom-0.8.4-3 (forgot to add this,corrected now - thx)

Regarding firmware try exploring /dev/input/by-path ( oddly enough I seem to have both 2.1.1 and 2.1.0. For me 2.1.0 works )
You can also look in the output of 
```

cat /proc/bus/input/devices

```

_**UPDATE**_ : I replaced the hid-*.ko files with the ones _nicolaasuni_ provided and now the Touch is much better than before . I would suggest you (and everyone else to try it : just make sure you backup you original driver files)

### Post by nema.arpit on 2009-11-01
@masafu
Langauage isn't that big a deal around here, no need to apologise :)

For **touch**, I suggest that you replace the hid-ntrig.ko and hid-wacom.ko from the first post.

**Right click with stylus**: 

     Try tapping the screen with the stylus with the button pressed.The context (right click menu) menu should pop-up ( I don't know how to get the context menu with just the stylus button pressed  - ask Ayuthia or Favux about this : they know more ).

Then you can press the stylus to the screen for some time and it'll give you a right click (like in windows)

> **bazmonkey said:**
> I'm having the same problem as masafu.  I did replace the two drivers per the instructions.  Touch works, but only if you actually touch and move your finger a bit.  Tapping to click, IOW, isn't quite working.  You gotta "drag a very small distance" instead.  Does that make sense?

While I'm on this thread, I just wanna throw out a few questions...

1) I have a tx2-1275dx.  I can't find specific threads about it.  This thread applies to me, yeah?

2) The touch turning off when the pen engages... that doesn't work, right?

Thanks guys.

This thread should work for you also.  All the tx2 series so far have come with the N-Trig touchscreen. The touch turning off when the pen engages does not work yet either.

As for your touch-click/drag, it almost sounds like either Rafi Rubin's patch was applied or else the hid-ntrig.ko file somehow did not get installed.  Also just to confirm, you are using 64-bit correct?  The one that was posted here was for 64-bit

If you are using 64-bit, you might try doing just the first two steps from this [guide]("http://linuxfans.keryxproject.org/?page_id=66").  Only do the first two steps though.  The rest of the steps will just be a repeat of what you have already done.

nicolaasuni - Do you know which patch was used for the hid-ntrig.ko file?

### Post by bazmonkey on 2009-11-02
> **Ayuthia said:**
> If you are using 64-bit, you might try doing just the first two steps from this [guide]("http://linuxfans.keryxproject.org/?page_id=66").  Only do the first two steps though.  The rest of the steps will just be a repeat of what you have already done.

Wow, thanks for the faster-than-fast response!  Funny you should mention that link, I just popped in to say I did that link and it *almost* worked.  After following the entire thing verbatim (though yes, it was repetitious), I ended up with the funkier behavior where no tapping worked (stylus or touch) and the cursor didn't follow anywhere near the device.  But I was happy to find the right-click working, so I started backing up the steps of that page, and switched my xorg.conf file back from the one included on that page.  It all seems to work now.

If only I could scroll by moving two fingers on the screen, I'd be in heaven.

Thanks again for the help!

### Post by olcan on 2009-11-05
[SIZE=2]Hello, I hope some[/SIZE][SIZE=2]one can please help me my microphone is not working. I have a [/SIZE][SIZE=2]TouchSmart tx2z-1000 CTO model no. FR171AV[/SIZE]. I have tried every step in the previous posts and nothing, here is my alsa-base.conf:
# autoloader aliases
install sound-slot-0 /sbin/modprobe snd-card-0
install sound-slot-1 /sbin/modprobe snd-card-1
install sound-slot-2 /sbin/modprobe snd-card-2
install sound-slot-3 /sbin/modprobe snd-card-3
install sound-slot-4 /sbin/modprobe snd-card-4
install sound-slot-5 /sbin/modprobe snd-card-5
install sound-slot-6 /sbin/modprobe snd-card-6
install sound-slot-7 /sbin/modprobe snd-card-7

# Cause optional modules to be loaded above generic modules
install snd /sbin/modprobe --ignore-install snd $CMDLINE_OPTS && { /sbin/modprobe --quiet --use-blacklist snd-ioctl32 ; /sbin/modprobe --quiet --use-blacklist snd-seq ; }
#
# Workaround at bug #499695 (reverted in Ubuntu see LP #319505)
install snd-pcm /sbin/modprobe --ignore-install snd-pcm $CMDLINE_OPTS && { /sbin/modprobe --quiet --use-blacklist snd-pcm-oss ; : ; }
install snd-mixer /sbin/modprobe --ignore-install snd-mixer $CMDLINE_OPTS && { /sbin/modprobe --quiet --use-blacklist snd-mixer-oss ; : ; }
install snd-seq /sbin/modprobe --ignore-install snd-seq $CMDLINE_OPTS && { /sbin/modprobe --quiet --use-blacklist snd-seq-midi ; /sbin/modprobe --quiet --use-blacklist snd-seq-oss ; : ; }
#
install snd-rawmidi /sbin/modprobe --ignore-install snd-rawmidi $CMDLINE_OPTS && { /sbin/modprobe --quiet --use-blacklist snd-seq-midi ; : ; }
# Cause optional modules to be loaded above sound card driver modules
install snd-emu10k1 /sbin/modprobe --ignore-install snd-emu10k1 $CMDLINE_OPTS && { /sbin/modprobe --quiet --use-blacklist snd-emu10k1-synth ; }
install snd-via82xx /sbin/modprobe --ignore-install snd-via82xx $CMDLINE_OPTS && { /sbin/modprobe --quiet --use-blacklist snd-seq ; }

# Load saa7134-alsa instead of saa7134 (which gets dragged in by it anyway)
install saa7134 /sbin/modprobe --ignore-install saa7134 $CMDLINE_OPTS && { /sbin/modprobe --quiet --use-blacklist saa7134-alsa ; : ; }
# Prevent abnormal drivers from grabbing index 0
options bt87x index=-2
options cx88_alsa index=-2
options saa7134-alsa index=-2
options snd-atiixp-modem index=-2
options snd-intel8x0m index=-2
options snd-via82xx-modem index=-2
options snd-usb-audio index=-2
options snd-usb-us122l index=-2
options snd-usb-usx2y index=-2
options snd-usb-caiaq index=-2
# Ubuntu #62691, enable MPU for snd-cmipci
options snd-cmipci mpu_port=0x330 fm_port=0x388
# Keep snd-pcsp from being loaded as first soundcard
options snd-pcsp index=-2
# Power down HDA controllers after 10 idle seconds
options snd-hda-intel power_save=10 power_save_controller=N
options snd-hda-intel model=acer-dmic
options snd-hda-intel model=3stack

[SIZE=2]On the sound preferences, the device that shows is [/SIZE]"Internal Audio Analog Stereo" and nothing happens with Mic1, Mic2 or Line-in.

I am very inexperienced with Linux, if you could please give me very clear instructions, I would very much appreciate it. Kernel version is 2.6.31.14-generic.

Thanks

### Post by Ayuthia on 2009-11-06
> **bazmonkey said:**
> Wow, thanks for the faster-than-fast response!  Funny you should mention that link, I just popped in to say I did that link and it *almost* worked.  After following the entire thing verbatim (though yes, it was repetitious), I ended up with the funkier behavior where no tapping worked (stylus or touch) and the cursor didn't follow anywhere near the device.  But I was happy to find the right-click working, so I started backing up the steps of that page, and switched my xorg.conf file back from the one included on that page.  It all seems to work now.

If only I could scroll by moving two fingers on the screen, I'd be in heaven.

Thanks again for the help!

I do have a version that currently does that (like the mouse wheel scroll so it is not smooth scrolling yet), but I am working with someone to help make sure that it is stable.  The driver is not the wacom driver though but something else that I have written.  It currently does not support the eraser yet and only works with the Vista firmware and the newest Win7 firmware.  The Win7 RC version (the one that provides four-finger touch) does not work with it.  This is because we have not figured out how to get more than one finger to be registered in Linux for that firmware.  The newest firmware release does produce the second finger but that is all it provides (for Windows and Linux).

### Post by armandhr on 2009-11-06
I have a tx2z-1000 CTO, and I just have to say thank you all for putting this how-to together.  After a combination of nicolaasuni's and nema.arpit's suggestions, my touchscreen and stylus are both working quite well!  I've modified the scripts a bit to set the autorotate to default to portrait rather than landscape mode, and I also added a button to toggle the touchscreen on and off (as recommended [here]("http://ubuntu-ky.ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1252492")), because I find that helps with note-taking.  I still have a couple of things to sort out on my system, but overall things are going well, so I'm very grateful to all of your scripts, patches, and advice. :)

> **olcan said:**
> [SIZE=2]Hello, I hope some[/SIZE][SIZE=2]one can please help me my microphone is not working. I have a [/SIZE][SIZE=2]TouchSmart tx2z-1000 CTO model no. FR171AV[/SIZE].
I have the exact same model, and I found that I didn't actually need the extra "options snd-hda-intel model=*" lines in alsa-base.conf in order for my sound to work, and they would in fact stop the internal microphone from working.  Now, granted, I'm using Kubuntu so my audio setup might be a little different than yours, but have you tried just commenting those lines out and rebooting?  If your sound still works, perhaps you'll then be able to see "Front mic" in alsamixer, which will allow you to follow the other steps that Ayuthia describes to get it working.  Otherwise, try changing the specific model to find one that works, i.e., "toshiba" or something.

Good luck!

---

The newest Win7 firmware does not work with this guide because the touch portion reports information differently so different patches are needed.  I am currently working on a couple of guides to help get this working.  Right now there is an experimental version [guide]("http://linuxfans.keryxproject.org/?page_id=77") available that you can try.  It has only been tested successfully with my laptop (tx2-1025dx).  We did test it with the Dell laptop with the Vista drivers, but the two-finger scrolling did not work.  The touch and pen worked though.  We are still working on the details on how to get the .fdi and xorg.conf files set up properly so that evdev does not take over a portion of the device.

Ok. That right-click touch feature has not been implemented yet.  I will need to put a timer in the code so that it can react to the touch for that.

As for the stylus, what is the resolution of your laptop?  Is it 1280x800?  I have not seen that issue in a while and it looks like X has only used the ntrig driver only.  

Also, are you using the 32-bit or 64-bit version of Ubuntu?  Also, can you check which version of the N-Trig driver you are using in Windows 7?  It should be able to be found in the Device Drivers portion of Windows 7.

### Post by Ayuthia on 2009-11-16
> **Astrofreak85 said:**
> Hi, I've got a 1310eg, and I've one problem with the touchscreen.
I've down the things from post #1, but now there are two issius:

1. The cursor has an ofset to the point where I touch on the screen, and it get further away as I move my finger on the screen.

2. I can't do a click, maybe this is related to the ofset problem.

Do I've to change something in the xorg.conf?
Screen got 1280x800 resolution

--Astro

It looks like your xorg.conf is not configured correctly so the system defaulted to evdev.  Did you update your xorg.conf to include the stylus and touch entries from the first post?  If so, do you know which version firmware you are using?  There is the one that comes with Vista, there is a Windows7 RC version, and then there is one that comes with Windows7.  Each firmware needs to be configured a little differently.

### Post by nema.arpit on 2009-11-19
Can you rotate the screen on command? eg try :
```

xrandr -o inverted
sleep 2s
xrandr -o normal


```Also check if hp-wmi is in lsmod :
```

lsmod | grep hp 

```it should give output with "hp_wmi" and "wmi" atleast
Also please post a copy of the script you are trying.

Hi, I've come over from the Touchsmart forums (TX2 1160ea) and just reinstalled Ubuntu Karmic 64 bit. The rotate button throws up an 'error while trying to run /rotate.sh' and the screen registers my finger / pen but just moves the cursor around, a couple of inches away, no clicks. Aside from that, everything else is working great.

### Post by Ayuthia on 2009-11-25
> **Franc Kaos said:**
> Hi, I've come over from the Touchsmart forums (TX2 1160ea) and just reinstalled Ubuntu Karmic 64 bit. My windows partition is 7 with n-trig 2.59 (Win7 RC version), and I followed the info on this thread to get touch working. The rotate button throws up an 'error while trying to run /rotate.sh' and the screen registers my finger / pen but just moves the cursor around, a couple of inches away, no clicks. Aside from that, everything else is working great.

I'm attaching (hopefully) my Xorg log file and I copy / pasted the /etc/X11/xorg.conf configuration on the first page, but I just don't know which path to follow from here. Any help gratefully received.

Franc Kaos.

(had to compress it, hope that's OK).

From your Xorg.0.log file, it is showing that it was unable to find the wacom module.  Have you compiled it yet?  As for why your cursor is a couple of inches away, it is because the evdev module has taken it.  It will cause the touchscreen to run in relative mode (like a mouse touchpad).

As for the device, since you are using Win7 firmware, it should be pointing to:
/dev/input/by-path/pci-0000:00:14.5-usb-0:2:1.1-event-mouse
instead.

---

### Post by Franc Kaos on 2009-11-27
> **Ayuthia said:**
> From your Xorg.0.log file, it is showing that it was unable to find the wacom module.  Have you compiled it yet?  As for why your cursor is a couple of inches away, it is because the evdev module has taken it.  It will cause the touchscreen to run in relative mode (like a mouse touchpad).

As for the device, since you are using Win7 firmware, it should be pointing to:
/dev/input/by-path/pci-0000:00:14.5-usb-0:2:1.1-event-mouse
instead.

Wow, after two days of thread reading, command line shenanigans, one reinstall (couldn't find the thread that discussed no boot up), I finally got so close. Xorg.0.log shows Wacom loading, used your dev/input line, and the cursor now sits underneath my finger, no click...

But of course, my kernel updated to .15 general (first update) and I noticed Digikid mentioning that over in the tx2 forum as breaking things. Do we get to go thru these 'fun' things on every kernel update? I'm sure I read -  somewhere, that .32 is gonna do something positive.

Anyway, feeling pretty good, learning loads, I can feel my brain slowly getting into gear, and will keep plugging away.

Franc Kaos.

Do what you will, this world's a fiction and is made up of contradiction.
'William Blake'

### Post by synace on 2009-12-17
this set of touch drivers seems like a regression from the patched 2.6.28 kernel drivers.

for example, if you turn off touch with "xsetwacom set touch touch off", it doesn't respond to touch anymore, but the digitizer doesn't work with your palm on the screen anymore in Xournal.

if this is what's going to be included w/ the next kernel, we still have some work to do!

the hp-wmi is working for me, thanks to all of that!

also, the middle panel button is working as well and is mapped as "XF86AudioMedia" keyboard button and I can reconfigure it via Keyboard Shortcuts.

### Post by henry_k on 2009-12-22
By combining post #1 and post #9 (by nicolaasuni and nema.arpit, respectively) I got things to work quite nicely on my HP tx2z 1244CA. However, there remained one BIG problem: I could not shutdown the computer. Others had the same problem. Now, I like to be a user more than a hacker and that would, it seems, to require of me to gain a better understanding of all the steps involved.

Most of the conversation on our forums is in the nature of trial and error. However, at age 82 I like to come to some definite answers. One would be to simply piggyback Ubuntu on Windows Vista or Windows 7 with the aid of Sun's VirtualBox, something that isn't quite to my taste. Another would be to do certain things using Windows (such as using the Windows version of the GIMP and reading ebooks with the screen turned 90 degrees), others using Ubuntu. Not very aesthetic and quite a hassle. I like Ubuntu because of its fine philosophy, its clean working environment, its way-superior mode of upgrading softwares, and its fast startup and shutdown procedures. To say nothing of the abundance of useful tools available free from sales pitches to wear you down.

It is with these thoughts in mind that I have some questions especially for a forum concerning running Ubuntu on a tx2 computer. Dumb questions to many of you, perhaps, but pretty fundamental to a non-geek.

1. My machine comes with an N-Trig digitizer, a product I understand to be a competitor of Wacom. And so I am puzzled by what Wacom is doing in these discussions. Nicolaasuni's post #1, begins (after installing Ubunti 9.10, etc.) by removing Wacom stuff:

Code: sudo apt-get purge wacom-tools xserver-xorg-input-wacom

but then immediately follows through by not only installing n-trig software (hid-ntrig.ko), but also Wacom stuff: hid-wacom.ko . So, what is the function of these two items? Moreover, I find myself soon faced with the question of which set is to be preferred, the set supplied by Nicolaasuni in post #4 (hid.tar.gz) or the set offered by nema.arpit in post #9 (hid-original.tar.gz). Just what makes one preferable to the other?

2. To confuse me further, I understand that the choice of hid-ntrig.ko also depends on whether we have Vista, Windows-7-RC, or Windows-7 firmware? Personally, I am totally at a loss here. Does our Ubuntu installation depend on what version of nWindows is installed? Or is the firmware referred to something that comes with the particular OEM versions of those operating system, which, I suppose would be different for HP and Dell?

3. Post #1 adds a number of sections to the xorg.conf script that involve the use of a Wacom driver. I take it then that the role of the Wacom software (hid-wacom.ko) is to permit rotation and autorotation of the screen. Has this addition other functions? And what is the meaning of the line "Option  "Button2"  "3"  #make side-switch a right button"? What is meant by side-switch and its being a right button?

4. We know that post #1 falls a little short in that the pointer on the screen is not synchronized with either stylus or finger-touch. I take it that it is for that reason that nema.armpit in post #9 advocates we install linuxwacom-0.8.4-3 (now meant to be 0.8.4-4). And indeed that solves the problem. But what are the roles of hid-wacom.ko and linuxwacom; are they both needed? I am totally non-plussed.

5. As one experiment of my own, I tried using linuxwacom-0.8.5-7 (the one under development at the time of this writing) and found that you could shutdown the machine! And so, the shutdown problem appears to lie in using version 0.8.4-4. But there is a price to pay for using 0.8.5-7: the starting up of the machine is hindered by what appears to be a stepping down of screen resolution (I may well have this wrong!) and so that be comes a pain in the butt.

6. And, then, there is still that little problem of finaggling the mouse pointer to stay up straight regardless of screen rotation....

Well, this post is long enough. I made it not only because I am looking for some answers, but also to demonstrate how many readers can get easily lost in the ongoing conversations. But nevertheless, I do, like many others do, very much appreciate the efforts of the individuals who do their utmost to make Ubuntu one hell of a nice operating system! 

Hat off to all.

---

### Post by Ayuthia on 2009-12-22
I am going to try my best to answer these questions.
> 
1. My machine comes with an N-Trig digitizer, a product I understand to be a competitor of Wacom. And so I am puzzled by what Wacom is doing in these discussions. Nicolaasuni's post #1, begins (after installing Ubunti 9.10, etc.) by removing Wacom stuff:

Code: sudo apt-get purge wacom-tools xserver-xorg-input-wacom

but then immediately follows through by not only installing n-trig software (hid-ntrig.ko), but also Wacom stuff: hid-wacom.ko . So, what is the function of these two items? Moreover, I find myself soon faced with the question of which set is to be preferred, the set supplied by Nicolaasuni in post #4 (hid.tar.gz) or the set offered by nema.arpit in post #9 (hid-original.tar.gz). Just what makes one preferable to the other?
I did not have a chance to review nema.arpit's version and the current version supplied in this thread.  However, I can tell you what the hid-ntrig.ko file does.  It is the actual driver that reads the information from the N-Trig digitizer and sends the information over to xorg (the desktop envirnoment controller).  The xorg driver that we use is currently made by Wacom (wacom_drv.so).  This driver takes the information from hid-ntrig.ko and sends the information to either operate a button, create a tap, or move the cursor.

The hid-wacom.ko is there mainly for wacom bluetooth devices.  It really should not be needed for the N-Trig devices but it is included at this time because nobody has tested it to see if it really is needed or not.

As for which hid-ntrig version to use, you can try nema-arpit's version.  I think that one might be the one that might have made modifications to at one time so that the touch will work better with the 0.8.4-X version of the Wacom driver.  I want to say that Nicolaasuni's version is the current version that is being used in 2.6.32 but the cursor does not always respond to finger taps on the screen.  However the 0.8.5-X versions seem to have fixed that issue.

> 2. To confuse me further, I understand that the choice of hid-ntrig.ko also depends on whether we have Vista, Windows-7-RC, or Windows-7 firmware? Personally, I am totally at a loss here. Does our Ubuntu installation depend on what version of nWindows is installed? Or is the firmware referred to something that comes with the particular OEM versions of those operating system, which, I suppose would be different for HP and Dell?
The hid-ntrig kernel module (hid-ntrig.ko) does rely on the firmware that is installed on the digitizer.  At this point, there has been one major release of the N-Trig firmware for each version of Windows that have been released.  So to answer your question, the hid-ntrig kernel module is currently dependent on which firmware that has been installed on the digitizer.  Each time N-Trig has released a new firmware version, the digitizer has reported information differently in Linux.  So the hid-ntrig module is dependent on how the firmware makes the digitizer report the data.  So each time N-Trig creates a new release, we have to see how it affects the kernel module and create patches if the new firmware made significant changes.

> 3. Post #1 adds a number of sections to the xorg.conf script that involve the use of a Wacom driver. I take it then that the role of the Wacom software (hid-wacom.ko) is to permit rotation and autorotation of the screen. Has this addition other functions? And what is the meaning of the line "Option  "Button2"  "3"  #make side-switch a right button"? What is meant by side-switch and its being a right button?As mentioned in number one, the hid-wacom.ko is for the bluetooth versions of Wacom devices.  It is actually the wacom_drv.so file that we are using for xorg.  The Wacom software helps control how to display the information on the screen.  It will convert the coordinate information when the screen is rotated, so that the cursor is moved to the correct portion of the screen.  The Button2 option is referring to the stylus.  There is a button on the stylus that when it is clicked, it will report it like a right mouse button click.

> 4. We know that post #1 falls a little short in that the pointer on the screen is not synchronized with either stylus or finger-touch. I take it that it is for that reason that nema.armpit in post #9 advocates we install linuxwacom-0.8.4-3 (now meant to be 0.8.4-4). And indeed that solves the problem. But what are the roles of hid-wacom.ko and linuxwacom; are they both needed? I am totally non-plussed.linuxwacom is the group that has been creating the kernel and xorg drivers for Wacom devices.  They created the hid-wacom.ko kernel module and the wacom_drv.so xorg module.  As I mentioned earlier, the differences between the nema.arpit's and the original poster's version are mainly with the finger taps.  When the pointer is not syncronized with the stylus or finger, it is usually because the wacom_drv.so xorg module or xorg.conf was not configured correctly so the wacom_drv.so xorg module could not work.  This causes the evdev xorg module (it is the generic xorg driver built to catch many types of devices like keyboards, mice, and tablets) to use the device.  The hid-ntrig.ko kernel module does not report the information the way that evdev likes it so evdev treats the screen like a mouse touchpad.

> 5. As one experiment of my own, I tried using linuxwacom-0.8.5-7 (the one under development at the time of this writing) and found that you could shutdown the machine! And so, the shutdown problem appears to lie in using version 0.8.4-4. But there is a price to pay for using 0.8.5-7: the starting up of the machine is hindered by what appears to be a stepping down of screen resolution (I may well have this wrong!) and so that be comes a pain in the butt.The 0.8.5-7 should not affect the screen resolution.  However, 0.8.5-7 most likely uses .fdi definitions instead of xorg.conf.  I can't remember if the patches for the N-Trig devices made it into 0.8.5-7 or not though.  I will have to try it out and see what happens with my Karmic build.  From what you are describing, it almost sounds like the wacom_drv.so xorg module is having problems disconnecting so your system is not shutting down.  You might try doing the following before shutting down and see if it allows you to shut down properly:
```
sudo modprobe -r hid-ntrig
```
This command unloads the hid-ntrig.ko kernel module and in theory should disconnect the wacom_drv.so xorg module so that it will not get stuck during the shut down process.  If it does fix it, we will need to see what is causing this problem on your computer.

> 6. And, then, there is still that little problem of finaggling the mouse pointer to stay up straight regardless of screen rotation....Is this issue with both versions of the linuxwacom code?  I have heard of this happening on some computers, but I cannot recall what was done to fix it.


Hopefully that helps answer some of your questions instead of thoroughly confusing you.  I can completely understand why you (and many others) have a lot questions about this.  As you might have guessed, the N-Trig device is pretty new in Linux so we are trying to get it working correctly right now.  Because of this, the N-Trig device does not work out of the box yet.  My guess is that we will not get this working out of the box until about Ubuntu 10.10 (unless another firmware release changes the information significantly again).  From what I have been reading, we might be getting an update from Stephane Chatty sometime in January or so and it will most likely not get merged into the kernel until 2.6.34 (which is most likely going to be after 10.04 is released).  And at this point linuxwacom 0.8.5 has not been released by the linuxwacom group as a stable driver yet so once that is released (which might be before 10.04 comes out) it will help in getting the device automatically configured.

I want to thank you for asking these questions.  It is nice to see someone with interest in understanding what these drivers do in Linux.

---

### Post by Ayuthia on 2009-12-26
> **henry_k said:**
> Ayuthia.

Re your concern about N-trig's version 2.153:

Upon checking, I found the following on my Windows 7 partition:
N-trig driver version: 1.26
N-trig firmware version: 0.0.0.0.0
N-trig software bundel version: 2.153

In other words, we may have somewhat different software bundles, yet the same driver.(?)

Is the location of that driver on the computer's CMOS chip as part of the BIOS? I am asking this question because with the HP upgrade to Windows 7, the HP instruction sheet that came with the upgrade software states that the "Upgrade Manager analyzes the BIOS and firmware installed on the computer. If necessary, the Upgrade Manager automatically updates the BIOS and firmware." Well, I looked at my BIOS and it told me that Vista is installed, not Windows 7. Kind of shakes my confidence in HP a bit.

The term "firmware" confuses me. I understood it to mean software burned into a chip.

H.

I am not sure about the exact location of the driver.  My understanding is that it is in the chip that is connected to the touchscreen and is not part of the BIOS.  My understanding of "firmware" is the same as yours.  There is a Windows application that will burn the software into the touchscreen chip.

As for the driver, there is a driver that Windows uses and it seems to be the same for Vista and for Windows 7 based on this [link]("http://lii-enac.fr/en/projects/shareit/ntrig.html").  However, from my understanding, Linux does not see that one.  It only has access to the N-Trig devices and communicates to the device via the firmware.  I am surprised about the version number of the firmware.  From the bundle perspective, I think that the bundle only packages the firmware and the Windows driver so the bundle version will change whenever there is an update to the firmware or the Windows driver.  Have you checked the System information in Windows 7?  If I recall correctly, there is a section in there that will tell you how many fingers it is able to read.  If it says two, then we know that you and I most likely have similar firmware running.  If it shows four or more, then it might be using the original firmware (since Windows it currently not showing a firmware version).

### Post by henry_k on 2009-12-28
Ayuthia.

I have followed the first route as per your suggestion. But I am missing a beat somewhere.

You wrote, "The patch can be found here." This gives me a version of hid-ntrig.ko which I understand to be a patch for kernel-2.6.31-16.

Am I correct assuming that there is no further need for patching linuxwacom-0.8.5-8/src/xdrv/wcmUSB.cc ? In post #66 you wrote: "Instead of trying 2.6.32, you might try the special patch that I supplied and use it with linuxwacom 0.8.5-7." The results of my efforts are disastrous: stylus and touch gone haywire.

To be clear, I have so far done the following on my tx2z 1244ca:

0. Via the HP Windows 7 upgrade to Vista, I updated the N-trig's software bundle from version 2.153 to version 2.184.

1. Freshly installed and updated Ubuntu 9.10 AMD64

2. Installed the proprietary hardware drivers "Broadcom STA wireless" and "ATI/AMD fglrx grapics."  I did not install the software modem driver and thus have no issue with sound that I am aware of. As a further aside: This time around I downloaded these drivers one right after the other and then installing them at the same time in my restart. It caused a hanging up of the machine! I had to pull out the battery to stop the machine from running.

3. Obtained and untarred your hid-ntrig-2.6.31-16-generic.tar.bz2. That gave me a version of hid-ntrig.ko

4. sudo apt-get purge wacom-tools xserver-xorg-input-wacom

5. Copied your hid-ntrig.ko to /lib/modules/`uname -r`/kernel/drivers/hid/
I did NOT copy any hid-wacom.ko to /lib/modules/`uname -r`/kernel/drivers/hid/  I understand this is not needed as was the case with earlier versions of the N-trig software bundel.True or false?

6. sudo depmod -a

7. Downloaded and untarred linuxwacom-0.8.5-8.tar.bz2

8. sudo apt-get update

9. sudo apt-get install build-essential libx11-dev libxi-dev x11proto-input-dev xserver-xorg-dev tk8.4-dev tcl8.4-dev libncurses5-dev

10. sudo apt-get upgrade

11. sudo apt-get install wacom-tools xserver-xorg-input-wacom

12. sudo apt-get purge wacom-tools xserver-xorg-input-wacom
[It is my understanding that step 11 puts in place some  software needed for the N-trig functioning and which that will notsubsequently  be purged in step 12, ??]

13. I did not (yet) patch linuxwacom-0.8.5-8/src.xdrv/wcmUSB.c with any n-trig.patch. I thought that versions 0.8.& eliminated the need for patching.

14. wget [http://kernel.ubuntu.com/git-repos/ubuntu/linux-2.6/drivers/hid/hid-ids.h](http://kernel.ubuntu.com/git-repos/ubuntu/linux-2.6/drivers/hid/hid-ids.h)

15. sudo cp ./hid-ids.h /lib/modules/`uname -r`/build/drivers/hid/hid-ids.h

16. cd linuxwacom-0.8.5-8
./configure --enable-wacom --prefix=/usr
make
sudo make install

17. Added hp-wmi and wacom to the script/etc/modules

18. Made my rotate.sh and autorotate.sh files executable and ensured to have autorotate.sh to my start-up file.

19. Replaced the /etc/X11.xorg.conf file by the one supplied by nicolaasuni in his post #1.

End result: fine rotations, but mouse initially pointing the wrong way with autorotate and stylus and touch totally beserk. 

(In a desparate move I did then add hid-wacom.ko, but that did not make a difference.)

### Post by Ayuthia on 2009-12-28
henry_k, I have just caught up with you and have found that 0.8.5-8 does not have the N-Trig changes in it.  I am currently testing this version and had to make changes to hid-ntrig.ko so a new patch will be coming out as soon as I can build the patches. ### Post by Ayuthia on 2009-12-28
henry_k, if you don't mind trying this [package]("http://linuxfans.keryxproject.org/packages/experimental/wacom/64-bit/prebuilt.tar.bz2").  To extract the file:
```
tar -xvjf prebuilt.tar.bz2
cd prebuilt
```
One of the files is the README.txt.  It contains the instructions on how to install it.

For others who would like to try it, it is only for the 2.6.31-16-generic kernel on 64-bit Ubuntu.  It contains the pre-build hid-ntrig.ko kernel module, a pre-patched linuxwacom-0.8.5-8 source, and hid-ids.h so that the linuxwacom source will compile.  The README.txt file also contains what I am currently using in my xorg.conf file for the wacom configuration.

If this works, I will create a new installation page that contains the instructions for the changes along with providing the patches for those who want to compile it manually.  I have tested this on my tx2 1025-dx laptop on 64-bit and have been able to rotate the screen and been able to use the stylus also.

EDIT:  It looks like I missed adding some .fdi files to the package.  I have attached them at the end of this post.  To extract this package:
```
tar -xvjf preprobe.tar.bz2
```
Now we need to create the folders in /etc/hal/fdi:
```
sudo mkdir /etc/hal/fdi/preprobe
sudo mkdir /etc/hal/fdi/preprobe/10osvendor
sudo mkdir /etc/hal/fdi/preprobe/20thirdparty
```If the directories are already there, an error message will appear and that is ok.  Now we can copy the files over to the preprobe directories:
```
sudo cp preprobe/10osvendor/10-x11-input.fdi /etc/hal/fdi/policy/10osvendor/
sudo cp preprobe/20thirdparty/10-ntrig.fdi /etc/hal/fdi/policy/20thirdparty/
```
Once that is complete you can try restarting your computer to see if it works.

### Post by henry_k on 2009-12-28
Ayuthia. 

Well, that is just great! And even more, I am mightely impressed with your deep knowledge of computer science. You are obviously not some amateur.

In the meantime, I'll just hold off for now before making another attempt.

This afternoon, I have also been looking a bit at the Windows-7 side of my installation. It is now my (amateurish)  understanding (and please let anyone correct me if I am wrong) that:

a. The tx2 motherboard has a ROM engineered by N-Trig. And that the contents of this ROM is not necessarily identical to that for, say a Dell tablet or the tablet of any other manufacturer. 

b. Much or all of the contents of this ROM (referred to as "firmware") is alterable by an updating program supplied by HP. Obviously, then, it requires a restart to install the associated drivers. From the Windows control panel, I learned that my firmware version is 4.5.31.8.5

c. I believe (again, do correct me!) that it is during start-up that this firmware checks and/or installs an N-trig software bundle in the Windows-7 operating system. My current bundle is version 2.184. One finds this bundle in Windows in 
C:\Program Files\N-trig\N-trig Software Bundle. Among the items in the bundle is a folder with stuff pertaining to the driver. The bundle contains many other programs mostly for the purpose of fine-tuning the tablet to suit the user. 

d. The driver version number found in Windows (now 1.268.33 on my computer) is a driver manufactured by Microsoft from info derived from the N-trig software bundle. Ubuntu/Linux volunteers are taking their own approach to come up with a proper driver. I believe this to be the hid-ntrig.ko patch to the Linux kernel. (Right or wrong?)

e. It would take time for Linux volunteers to catch up with the technology N-trig supplied to Microsoft. Their principal efforts now concern recognizing the N-trig tablet, its autorotation and 90-deg rotation, the proper use of mouse, stylus and touch.

f. Linux volunteers apparently already had some considerable experience with Wacom tablets and by luck of circumstance managed to graft this onto their efforts to make the N-trig digitizer rotate, etc. This, I suspect now, is the function of the linuxwacom software. (Right or wrong?)

g. It is my impression that when N-trig changed from version 2.153 (which still came with the Windows-7 upgrade) to version 2.184, they changed something that stopped linuxwacom from functioning as intended. Hence, the latest effort is to replace the wacom driver referred to in the xorg.conf file, as offered by Nicolaasuni in his post #1) by a ntrig driver that Ayuthia (and perhaps also others) has been and still is working on.

h. Although we now can only do the 90-deg rotation by hand, so to speak, I understand that volunteers are directing attention to the use of the rotation button on the bezel of the tablet's screen. Kind of a nice finishing touch.

i. As for the handwriting aspect of things, it appears to me that Windows has right now the lead in the quality of their handwriting recognation capabilities. On the other hand, Linux has an interesting assortment of recognition programs.

ALL IN ALL A MIGHTY IMPRESSIVE SHOW BY THE WORLDWIDE COMMUNITY OF LINUX VOLUNTEERS. AWSOME.

H.

---

### Post by Ayuthia on 2009-12-28
> **henry_k said:**
> Ayuthia. 

Well, that is just great! And even more, I am mightely impressed with your deep knowledge of computer science. You are obviously not some amateur.
Thank you!  My degree was in computer science and I enjoy tinkering with the computer to see it do different things.  Based on the knowledge that I have seen in the Linux world, I still do feel like an amateur.

> 
a. The tx2 motherboard has a ROM engineered by N-Trig. And that the contents of this ROM is not necessarily identical to that for, say a Dell tablet or the tablet of any other manufacturer. 

I am in agreement with you on this one.  From what I have seen, the firmware versions have not matched between the two devices.  Also the firmware have not been released at the same time for Dell and HP.

> 
b. Much or all of the contents of this ROM (referred to as "firmware") is alterable by an updating program supplied by HP. Obviously, then, it requires a restart to install the associated drivers. From the Windows control panel, I learned that my firmware version is 4.5.31.8.5

Correct.
> 
c. I believe (again, do correct me!) that it is during start-up that this firmware checks and/or installs an N-trig software bundle in the Windows-7 operating system. My current bundle is version 2.184. One finds this bundle in Windows in 
C:\Program Files\N-trig\N-trig Software Bundle. Among the items in the bundle is a folder with stuff pertaining to the driver. The bundle contains many other programs mostly for the purpose of fine-tuning the tablet to suit the user. 
I am not too sure about when the N-Trig software gets installed.  But you are correct about what the bundle contains.

> 
d. The driver version number found in Windows (now 1.268.33 on my computer) is a driver manufactured by Microsoft from info derived from the N-trig software bundle. Ubuntu/Linux volunteers are taking their own approach to come up with a proper driver. I believe this to be the hid-ntrig.ko patch to the Linux kernel. (Right or wrong?)
This is correct.

> 
e. It would take time for Linux volunteers to catch up with the technology N-trig supplied to Microsoft. Their principal efforts now concern recognizing the N-trig tablet, its autorotation and 90-deg rotation, the proper use of mouse, stylus and touch.

This is also correct.  Microsoft gets some information to help get the hardware to work where Linux does not have this luxury.  Some of the things that you mentioned are HP-related items and not N-Trig (like the rotation).  We still are trying to figure out how to gain access to some of the bezel buttons and activate the led light for the mute button.  We have figured out how to determine when the tablet is rotated and have created an application for the auto-rotation, but it would be nice to get it incorporated like other tablets.

> 
f. Linux volunteers apparently already had some considerable experience with Wacom tablets and by luck of circumstance managed to graft this onto their efforts to make the N-trig digitizer rotate, etc. This, I suspect now, is the function of the linuxwacom software. (Right or wrong?)
This is correct.  There is a lot of code that was already there for the HP laptops that have the Wacom hardware so it was not too hard to create the driver to send the information in the same way.  However, there is a possibility that this will be switching over to the evdev driver.  Based on the stuff that is being reported from ENAC, they are able to get the multi-touch devices to work with xorg-server 1.7 and evdev.  I have not had a chance to try it out yet.

> 
g. It is my impression that when N-trig changed from version 2.153 (which still came with the Windows-7 upgrade) to version 2.184, they changed something that stopped linuxwacom from functioning as intended. Hence, the latest effort is to replace the wacom driver referred to in the xorg.conf file, as offered by Nicolaasuni in his post #1) by a ntrig driver that Ayuthia (and perhaps also others) has been and still is working on.
Yes.  The four(or more)-finger version that was provided in the Win7 Release Candidate only reported one-finger in Linux.  I am not for sure if the developers at ENAC have figured out how to turn on the multi-touch for it yet.  So the previous firmware versions have been reporting things as one finger and the second finger was the width and height based on the distance away from the first finger.  However, the newest version automatically reports as multi-finger (in the same way as it is reported in Windows) so we needed to make the modifications to make it report the fingers correctly.

> h. Although we now can only do the 90-deg rotation by hand, so to speak, I understand that volunteers are directing attention to the use of the rotation button on the bezel of the tablet's screen. Kind of a nice finishing touch.This is also correct.  We have created an auto-rotate script that has been working, but it is not the same as how other tablets rotation signals are being picked up.  We have not been able to activate the rotate button in the tx2 tablets (at least in my version) yet.

> i. As for the handwriting aspect of things, it appears to me that Windows has right now the lead in the quality of their handwriting recognation capabilities. On the other hand, Linux has an interesting assortment of recognition programs.
This is also correct.  I have been impressed with how the handwriting recognition works with Windows.  It would be nice to be able to get this improved in Linux, but I have no idea about where to start with that and I am not for sure about how much I would need to learn to understand how to convert handwriting into characters.

> 
ALL IN ALL A MIGHTY IMPRESSIVE SHOW BY THE WORLDWIDE COMMUNITY OF LINUX VOLUNTEERS. AWSOME.

I agree with you on this!  It is very impressive about how many people have contributed to Linux and I am very grateful for all the things that they have provided. I just hope that I am able to help spread the joy that they have provided for me.

---

### Post by armandhr on 2009-12-29
> **Ayuthia said:**
> henry_k, if you don't mind trying this [package]("http://linuxfans.keryxproject.org/packages/experimental/wacom/64-bit/prebuilt.tar.bz2").  To extract the file:
```
tar -xvjf prebuilt.tar.bz2
cd prebuilt
```
One of the files is the README.txt.  It contains the instructions on how to install it.

For others who would like to try it, it is only for the 2.6.31-16-generic kernel on 64-bit Ubuntu.  It contains the pre-build hid-ntrig.ko kernel module, a pre-patched linuxwacom-0.8.5-8 source, and hid-ids.h so that the linuxwacom source will compile.  The README.txt file also contains what I am currently using in my xorg.conf file for the wacom configuration.

If this works, I will create a new installation page that contains the instructions for the changes along with providing the patches for those who want to compile it manually.  I have tested this on my tx2 1025-dx laptop on 64-bit and have been able to rotate the screen and been able to use the stylus also.Ayuthia, I have tested this package, and I am pleased to say that I can now shutdown my computer just fine.  I do have a few comments, however.  First, there is a slight typo in the README:
```
sudo cp hid-ntrig.ko /lib/mdules/$(uname -r)/kernel/drivers/hid/
``` should be ```
sudo cp hid-ntrig.ko /lib/modules/$(uname -r)/kernel/drivers/hid/
```

> **Ayuthia said:**
> EDIT:  It looks like I missed adding some .fdi files to the package.  I have attached them at the end of this post.  To extract this package:
```
tar -xvjf preprobe.tar.bz2
```
Now we need to create the folders in /etc/hal/fdi:
```
sudo mkdir /etc/hal/fdi/preprobe
sudo mkdir /etc/hal/fdi/preprobe/10osvendor
sudo mkdir /etc/hal/fdi/preprobe/20thirdparty
```If the directories are already there, an error message will appear and that is ok.I already had the /etc/hal/fdi/preprobe directory, but I needed to create the two subdirectories.  Also, I am assuming that by
```
sudo cp preprobe/10osvendor/10-x11-input.fdi /etc/hal/fdi/policy/10osvendor/
sudo cp preprobe/20thirdparty/10-ntrig.fdi /etc/hal/fdi/policy/20thirdparty/
```
you meant
```
sudo cp preprobe/10osvendor/10-x11-input.fdi /etc/hal/fdi/preprobe/10osvendor/
sudo cp preprobe/20thirdparty/10-ntrig.fdi /etc/hal/fdi/preprobe/20thirdparty/
```
Is this correct?  Or did you intend for the 10osvendor and 20thirdparty directories to be created in the policy directory rather than the preprobe directory?

Finally, everything (stylus, touchscreen, tap-to-click, rotation, shutdown, suspend & resume) seems to work perfectly, with one exception:  when the screen is rotated (to anything other than normal orientation), the touch doesn't respond quite right.  Namely, after touching the screen the mouse jumps to some point on the left side of the screen.  However, stylus works fine, and whenever I have the screen rotated it usually means I want to use the stylus anyway, so it's not a big deal.  I just thought I'd mention it.

Thanks for all the support.

### Post by henry_k on 2009-12-30
To digitalchemystery.

To be sure that we are all on the same page, I consider it important that we clearly state what version of N-trig driver we are using. A few posts ago, Ayuthia pointed out that there possibly is a difference between versions 2.153 and 2.184. It may well be that it is also important what version firmware we have.

I found, after upgrading from Vista to Windows 7 on my tx2z 1244ca, that I had N-trig version 2.153 and had to go to the Hewlett-Packard website to obtain version 2.184 which I understand Ayuthia to be using. The firmware version became 4.5.31.8.5.

A number of posts refer to what Windows version there exist on the tx2 as well as a model number of the tx2. But it seems to me that the most relevant info is the version of the firmware and of the N-trig driver. That information is found via the Windows control panel.

It looks like we shall be going through some months of turmoil with changing versions not only of N-trig firmware and N-trig driver, but also upgrades of the kernel used in Ubuntu. The risk is that we keep on confusing apples with oranges. It seems to me advisable that each post on this forum states at the outside these parameters.

H.

Aside to Ayuthia. I am about to give your latest package (post #75) a try. Just want to find out first about .fdi files and evdev. Talk about lifelong learning!

### Post by henry_k on 2009-12-30
Ayuthia 

Re your post #75 (& notes by armandhr's post #81),_** things are just about where they should be and I am pleased as Punch.**_

As you know, I am using N-trig version 2.184; firmware package version 4.5.31.8.3 .
My computer is a tx2s 1244ca and upon buying I was entitled to an upgrade from Vista to Windows 7.

For the benefit for all lurkers on this forum, this is what I did before following Ayuthia's post #75:  

a. I redid the opgrade. First I reinstalled Windows 7 from my 2-disk set and found it did not contain any reference to the N-trig tablet at all. Nor was I able to rotate the screen and use the buttons on the right side of the screen's bezel. Not surprising, of course.

b. I then ran the HP Upgrade Manager disk (which officially should be run first). That installed specific HP stuff, to wit:
- driver for HP QuickLaunch Buttons (v. 6.50.3.1)
- driver for Radeon HD3200 Graphics Video (v. 1.00)
- driver for Realtek High Definition Audio (v. 1.00)
- application: HP Wireless Assistant (v. 3.50.7.2) (Not sure why we need that.)
- application: HP Support Assistant (v. 4.1.11.3) (Still have to figure out the benefit of that one.)
- application: Digital Personal Fingerprint Reader for Consumer NB (v. 4.1.11.3) (I haven't dared yet to use fingerprint recognition for fear that if something is wrong I cannot get at my harddrive. Probably an overdose of paranoia on my part.)
- application: Muvee Reveal (v. 7.0.40.10061) (Another goodie I haven't tasted yet.)
- application: Recovery Manager Installation Patch for Windows 7 (v.1.00) (Well, I had already installed Windows 7 and so it could take a day off.)
- application: Patch for DOT4 Runtime Drivers (v. 1.0) (Not a clue what this is about.)
- application: N-Trig DuoSense Digital Sofware/Driver _(v. 2.1.5.3_)

c. Subsequently I reinstalled the root part of Ubuntu 9.10 AMD64 and updated it. I then installed the Broadcom STA wireless driver and after rebooting installed also ATI's fglrx driver. As for not installing the proprietary Software Modem driver, see my post #86. 

d. I still had on my computer the scripts for rotate.sh and autorotate.sh, but I had to do 
henry@hp:~$ sudo gedit /etc/modules 
in order to add hp-wmi and wacom. They are both needed.

e. I then followed the instructions found in Ayuthia;s post #75 with the corrections by armandhr (post #81). As for the xorg.conf file, I simply added to the existing file the lines provided by Ayuthia.

f. I made rotate.sh and authorotate.sh (which I keep in a folder I named HPtx2 ) executable:
henry@hp:~/HPtx2$ chmod +x rotate.sh
henry@hp:~/HPtx2$ chmod +x autorotate.sh
and made sure the autorotate.sh application runs at start up as per Nicolaasuni's post #1 by adding the autorotate.sh script to *System > Preferences > Startup Applications*.
I found it convenient to add the application rotate.sh to the right side of the bottom panel. One tick wit the stylus does the trick.

If you were to restart, like I did out of curiosity, with N-trig version 2.153 then you will be disappointed because the pointing devices go haywire. Therefore:

g. Upgrade, via Windows, the N-trig driver to version 2.184.

I got fine results. Even the button on the stylus worked as it should. There is only one little item to be solved: the pointer (mouse, stylus, touch) points in the direction of the last screen orientation. For now, I solved the problem by placing an empty textfile on the desktop. Once I move my pointer over that file, the pointer behaves properly.

And so, a small step by Ayuthia, a big step for tx2/Ubuntu fans.

H. 

P.S. Obviously, I need some time to check out all the nooks and crannies in my tablet setup and its uses.

### Post by digitalchemystery on 2010-01-01
> **henry_k said:**
> How do you update drivers for the Windows 7 operating system in Ubuntu and how do you verify what version N-trig driver is installed?

The drivers have no bearing on this -- they're software, and they're OS-specific. The real question is the firmware. Unless you're on an OS which is binary-compatible with Windows and uses the same driver model, any new driver release is essentially worthless. N-Trig's software provided by HP bundles driver updates and firmware updates. I don't quite know enough about the process of direct device access, but I image it would be possible to do a firmware update from Linux after extracting the contents of the provided installer.

I'd like some help tracking down some remaining issues and one emergent one.

The shutting down issue seems to be a problem with 0.8.4-4.  It seems to be fixed in 0.8.5-x series, but the patches for this set has been a moving target because of all the changes that are being applied (this set is still in development stages).

The right-click is set with by setting Button2 to 3 for the stylus in xorg.conf:
```
        Option "Button2" "3"
```I can't remember if the current unpatched kernel version has a problem with the click or not.  I just recall that there is one additional patch that is applied in 2.6.32 that is not in the Karmic kernel.

As for the sliding touch, that is an issue with the current kernel.  I think that this issue gets fixed in 0.8.5-x series.  The patches that I supplied did fix it, but for some reason it appears that you have problems with the patch.

As for the touch toggling, it sounds like another driver is also using the device.  You can check xinput to see if there is an additional entry that is assigned to the touchscreen.  Xorg.0.log might also tell you something.

### Post by digitalchemystery on 2010-01-08
As a matter of thanks for the help/work of others, here's a simple touch-toggle script that makes use of notify-send.

If you don't have notify-send, install it with
```

# apt-get install libnotify-bin

```

Now, you can create a launcher for this script, assign it a hotkey/corner or whatever works best for you. Make it a voice command. Assign it to one of the bezel buttons. Go crazy.

```

#!/bin/sh
STATUS=`xsetwacom get touch touch`
if [ "$STATUS" == "0" ]
  then
    notify-send -i dialog-info "Touch enabled"
    xsetwacom set touch touch on
else
    notify-send -i dialog-info "Touch disabled"
    xsetwacom set touch touch off
fi

```

---

Pen works very reliably and consistently with me... but right clicking with the pen and functioning touch are *very* transient. When it works it works until the next reboot.

Something which has tended to work (but isn't currently):

Recopy the patched ntrig module to where it belongs
# sudo cp /usr/src/linux-2.6.31/drivers/hid/hid-ntrig.ko /lib/modules/`uname -r`/kernel/drivers/hid

Throw in depmod for good measure
# depmod -a

Unload the n-trig module (tends to render system inoperable)
# modprobe -r hid_ntrig

At this point, I kill the power, reboot, and hope the touch/right-click decided to work. There's *nothing* right about that. I don't want to be a complaining user ... help me help get this fixed? Oh, and it would be nice to be able to reliably suspend/resume and shutdown without the physical switch!

I am not for sure about where to begin! :) I think the best place to start is to see what Xorg.0.log.old says when things don't work.  You might try using the evdev driver to see if it crashes or not.  I know that in Lucid, the evdev driver seems to work with the N-Trig device and that is what ENAC uses for their demos that have been posted on the internet.  If the evdev driver works, then we know it is an issue with the Wacom driver.  However, if it crashes with the evdev driver also, then it is most likely a kernel issue.  I have found in some odd cases for the 32-bit version, if a certain variable type is defined in two lines like:
```
int X, Y;
int Z;
```
There have been some problems with the 32-bit working.  Once I converted X, Y, and Z to one line:
```
int X, Y, Z;
```all was fine.

Like I said earlier, Xorg.0.log.old might be able to provide some clues about what happened when things crash.  I have seen the drivers trying to unload and then they get stuck.  Unfortunately, I have not found the solution to the problem, but sometimes bug reports in Launchpad may help.

You might first start with looking at /dev/hidrawX, where X is an integer.  For me, it is /dev/hidraw1 because I am using the Win7 firmware, but I have seen people report any number between 0-3.  The hidrawX hexdump (or xxd) will help determine what codes come over.  The other thing that you could do is put printk statements in the ntrig_event function in drivers/hid/hid-ntrig.c source.  There is most likely a statement there in my patch source to show what code is being sent by the firmware/device.  From there, you will most likely be able to determine what is being sent over from your N-Trig device.  If you have any questions about this, feel free to either PM me or reply here and I can try to explain further.  There is a chance that you and I are using different firmware versions and that could be causing problems when the kernel module is reading from the device.

Hopefully this is not too confusing and might help you.  Like I said, feel free to either reply here or send me a PM and I can try to help explain things further.  It is always helpful to have another set of eyes.  I also have a computer science background and have been trying to understand the kernel module code.  I am getting better, but I know that there are several people that know more than me (like Rafi Rubin and Stephane Chatty).  It is fun reading/translating the data that is coming from the device though.

### Post by Ayuthia on 2010-01-09
> **digitalchemystery said:**
> Eh, no joy with the evdev driver. It just didn't work. The Xorg.0.log showed it being loaded, but the cursor doesn't move and it appears to not register touch.If that is the case, you might need to check /dev/hidraw0 to see if any data is coming out of it.  If there isn't anything, then the hid-ntrig driver is not working.  

> My little recopy/depmod/crash sequence isn't working any more.
Commenting the n-trig section of 10-linuxwacom brings me back to the days of incorrect finger/stylus tracking and no clicking whatsoever. 
This means that the evdev driver is taking it whenever you comment the n-trig section.
> Sometimes commenting out the device sections in xorg.conf, rebooting, commenting them, and rebooting works. Not always, so it's probably coincidental. One time it worked with the 10-linuxwacom commented out, but the behavior went back to 'normal' (for the commented .fdi per above) on the next reboot. Srsly... whiskey. tango. foxtrot.

I guess it's time to learn about writing kernel modules. I'll start with your lead in hid-ntrig.c and see if I can figure out how to compile a single module ... otherwise I'll have to rebuild all of those hid modules with each revision.

Can you think of anything I might have f'd up in HAL/Xorg configuration? I'm not sure exactly how those magic FDIs work, where they come in, what they replace, what is working, and what is broken. I'll try to hunt down a copy of somone's 90-ntrig.fdi and see if I can use that to replace my entries in xorg.conf. Yeah, that, then coding as a fallback.

I am not for sure if it is anything to do with the driver or not.  For me, this version of the Win7 firmware has not worked as well as the other versions.  I have had to go into Win7 and reset the touch portion several times because I would get "dead" spots on the screen.

You might also check lshal to see which driver is being loaded from the .fdi files.  From your previous post, it looks like synaptics might have started using your device.  It could also be possible that your system is crashing because of another driver not liking what is being sent to it.

The Win7 firmware seems to need more configuring than the other firmware because of how the system sees it configured.  I have had to do add the other .fdi files so that other drivers would not take the device and verify it though lshal to make sure that Hal was not trying to assign the device.  All I can say is that I am happy that I am a KDE user and not a Gnome user in this scenario because I am also testing things out in Lucid.  Lucid currently has Hal removed so lshal is not installed.  That has left me trying to learn how udev rules are used.  Fun stuff.

So to recap, if your cursor does not follow your finger but it moves, it means that another driver is using the device (either evdev or synaptics).  You can most likely see that in Xorg.0.log and can most likely confirm which driver is using it through lshal.  If the cursor does not move at all, it most likely means that either the wacom driver does not recognize the device or else the hid-ntrig driver is not working.   You can confirm this by checking /dev/hidrawX (/dev/hidraw0 in your case).

Your information has been different than the others and it has left me curious about what your device is reporting.  If you don't mind, would you post a portion of:
```
sudo xxd -g1 /dev/hidraw0
```Please use five fingers on the screen so that we can tell how many the firmware is able to report.  Hopefully it will tell us what is happening.
