### Post by digitalchemystery on 2010-01-09
Another clean reboot, and it works again... cool.

New observations are in:

- Restart worked. System with functioning touch, stylus, and right-click
- Shut down required manual intervention.
- First start-up after manual shut-down brings system up touchless and right-clickless
- A successful restart restored touch, stylus, and right-click

Attempting to break it in a consistent way...

I'll do the following:

- Restart and check for functioning triad
- Attempt system shut-down, expecting to do improper shut-down
- Iff improper shut-down, check triad on next boot, expecting stylus tracking
- Iff symptoms present, perform normal restart
- Iff functional triad ... cool, reproducible results.


So, now that I'm pretty close to having this working like it should have 3~ months ago, time to push it a little.

I've got the fingerprint reader working. A little fine-tuning is in line, and I've downloaded a copy of the source the repo, so I'll see how approachable what I'm hoping to create would be at this point. Anyway, it kind of works, and it's about 30% reliable. Some improvement would be nice, but, again, what works seems to work well. The one of the first steps would be to figure out how to automatically authorize the key-ring when logging in based on fingerprint recognition.

Audio works, but plugging in headphones doesn't stop playback on the external speakers. That almost defeats the purpose of headphones.

My system lists microphones, but I don't see their activity indicators moving, so more might be needed there. A brief shot at audacity didn't register any sound. The dual mics is a cool feature ... more possibilities in signal processing if I understand correctly.

I'm also hoping to find a way to modify gnome-panel to use true transparency when using a compositing manager. The compiz opacity hack doesn't lessen the problem by much, as the wallpaper gets painted on the supposedly-transparent panel. It creates a sloppy, unsightly effect. The transparency hack has the unfortunate side-effect of making the text and icons on the panel dimmer (compiz changes the opacity of the entire panel). Gnome-terminal understands transparency correctly. I've got the source for that one. It's quite difficult for me to understand what's going on in some of these projects at times -- different coders thinking differently writing different things in different languages. I'm hoping to clone gnome-terminal's implementation and graft it onto gnome-panel, removing the infected portion. Unless someone has (hopefully) beaten me to it.

I'm hoping for some progress on rotation, multi-touch (in addition to learning more about multi-input X), shut-down, and touch-and-hold as right-click.

### Post by henry_k on 2010-01-09
Re:_ Recent Ubuntu update to kernel 2.6.31-17-generic_

For the benefit of HP tx2 users without computer science background and further to my post #87, I did the following to get my rotation, etc. working again:

sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get build-dep xserver-xorg-input-wacom
sudo cp hid-ntrig.ko /lib/mdules/$(uname -r)/kernel/drivers/hid/
sudo cp hid-ids.h /lib/modules/$(uname -r)/build/drivers/hid/
 
I do not have a background in computer science and so it may well be that the first two steps are redundant.

At the time of this writing, [http://linuxwacom.sourceforge.net/](http://linuxwacom.sourceforge.net/) shows that linuxwacom has been updated from version 0.8.5-8 to 0.8.5-9, but because things worked I left well enough alone.

My computer is an HP tx2 1244ca. I am using N-trig version 2.184; firmware package version 4.5.31.8.3 .

H.

sudo apt-get build-dep xserver-xorg-input-wacom
sudo cp hid-ntrig.ko /lib/mdules/$(uname -r)/kernel/drivers/hid/
sudo cp hid-ids.h /lib/modules/$(uname -r)/build/drivers/hid/
 
I do not have a background in computer science and so it may well be that the first two steps are redundant.


The first step should be the only one needed (iff you already have xserver-xorg-input-wacom). It definitely doesn't hurt to do, as you've found out  :D

> 
At the time of this writing, [http://linuxwacom.sourceforge.net/](http://linuxwacom.sourceforge.net/) shows that linuxwacom has been updated from version 0.8.5-8 to 0.8.5-9, but because things worked I left well enough alone.

My computer is an HP tx2 1244ca. I am using N-trig version 2.184; firmware package version 4.5.31.8.3 .

H.

I noticed the update, but I haven't looked into Ayuthia's patch for 0.8.5-8, and it doesn't work for 0.8.5-9. It might be trivial to fix, but, again, I haven't dug into that.

That's cool that it's working for you ... consistently, reliably? Every reboot for me is Russian roulette ... but I've been able to get it functional within a couple of reboots.

The first time you run the script, it will disable touch and show a notification. ### Post by digitalchemystery on 2010-01-09
The rotate script is just for rotating display/devices, and it doesn't bind to the rotate button like I thought it might. I'll look into using the bezel buttons and report progress as I make it.

While we wait, here's a simple random-wallpaper script I wrote in python. I've seen ones as bash scripts before, but I'm much more comfortable with python.

```

#!/usr/bin/python
WALLPAPERS="/path/to/you/wallpapers"

if __name__=="__main__":
    import os, random

    # make a list of wallpapers in the directory
    wallpapers = [wallpaper for wallpaper in os.listdir(WALLPAPERS) if os.path.splitext(wallpaper)[-1] in ('.jpg', '.png', '.svg')]

    # choose a random number in the range of the number of wallpapers
    choice = random.randrange(1, len(wallpapers))
    
    # set the wallpaper to the randomly-selected wallpaper
    os.system('gconftool-2 --set /desktop/gnome/background/picture_filename -t string "%s"' % 
              os.path.join(WALLPAPERS,wallpapers[choice]))

```

Alright, back to work

### Post by Ayuthia on 2010-01-10
> **digitalchemystery said:**
> 
I've attached the output of the 5-finger test. I started capture, touched with all 5 simultaneously, 4, 3, 2, 1. Then back to 5 and a bit of repositioning/sliding the fingers. Then 2-finger pinch and 2-finger expand. Plenty of data.

It's a bz2'd text file.

From what I can tell, the data that you sent seems to report like the Vista firmware.  It looks like it reports the first and second finger without an id attached. Also, your device is pointing to /dev/hidraw0 which is also the same as how the Vista firmware reports.

So when I reformatted the data you posted, the information looked like this (I am only posting the first few lines):
```

00 02 07 47 21 50 0d 62 04 00 00                       
00 02 03 47 21 4f 0d 63 04 3d 08                       
00 02 03 48 21 51 0d 69 04 a0 08                       
00 02 03 48 21 51 0d 56 04 98 08                       
00 02 03 42 21 50 0d 54 04 93 08                       
00 02 03 3b 21 4d 0d 54 04 7d 08                       
00 02 03 32 21 50 0d 4b 04 ae 08                       
00 02 03 29 21 4f 0d 55 04 7f 08                       
00 02 03 1f 21 4c 0d 4b 04 98 08                       
00 02 03 e2 20 4a 0d a6 11 15 0b                       
00 02 03 a9 20 ff 0b ab 11 c4 0a                       
00 02 03 76 20 de 0a a6 11 e4 0a                       
00 02 03 49 20 e5 09 a8 11 c5 0a                       
00 02 03 22 20 0b 09 9b 11 4b 15        

```
So to translate the first line, the 00 02 07 (0x0702 in hex) shows that it sees one finger because 00 02 03 usually shows as two-finger mode.  The 47 21 is the X coordinate of the first finger (0x2147 hex or 8519 in decimal) and 50 0d is the Y coordinate of the first finger (0x0d50 hex or 3048 in decimal).  The 62 04 and 00 00 is part of the second finger (0x0462 hex or 1122 in decimal).  The part that I am not sure about is if this is the width/height or if it is supposed to be a set of coordinates.  The thing that confuses me is that some of the single finger reports show a large number in the width/height section.

Unfortunately, this does not help explain why you are experiencing the shutdown/restart issue.

### Post by henry_k on 2010-01-10
> **digitalchemystery said:**
> As a matter of thanks for the help/work of others, here's a simple touch-toggle script that makes use of notify-send.

If you don't have notify-send, install it with
```

# apt-get install libnotify-bin

```Now, you can create a launcher for this script, assign it a hotkey/corner or whatever works best for you. Make it a voice command. Assign it to one of the bezel buttons. Go crazy.

```

#!/bin/sh
STATUS=`xsetwacom get touch touch`
if [ "$STATUS" == "0" ]
  then
    notify-send -i dialog-info "Touch enabled"
    xsetwacom set touch touch on
else
    notify-send -i dialog-info "Touch disabled"
    xsetwacom set touch touch off
fi

```

There appears to be a little slip-up here. I changed the line
if [ "$STATUS" == "0" ]
to
if [ "$STATUS" = "0" ]

Also, because the notification is so much delayed, I commented out the notify-send commands. 

Thanks for your contribution.

Moreover, my first impression is that Window's handwriting recognition software functions better than what is available for Linux. 

Henceforth it is that ,"thanks to Windows," I have a double-booting system and it is with Ayuthia's help, and also digitalchemystery's contribution of post #105 that I have a fine tx2 tablet that functions quite satisfactorily under Linux.

Here is hoping I have not made a fool of myself.

### Post by Ayuthia on 2010-01-10
> **digitalchemystery said:**
> Another clean reboot, and it works again... cool.

New observations are in:

- Restart worked. System with functioning touch, stylus, and right-click
- Shut down required manual intervention.
- First start-up after manual shut-down brings system up touchless and right-clickless
- A successful restart restored touch, stylus, and right-click

Attempting to break it in a consistent way...

I'll do the following:

- Restart and check for functioning triad
- Attempt system shut-down, expecting to do improper shut-down
- Iff improper shut-down, check triad on next boot, expecting stylus tracking
- Iff symptoms present, perform normal restart
- Iff functional triad ... cool, reproducible results.


So, now that I'm pretty close to having this working like it should have 3~ months ago, time to push it a little.

I am trying to review your issue a little.  Going back with the modprobe -r and modprobe commands, I usually will shut down X from the console (control-alt-F1) before removing the modules:
```
sudo service gdm stop
sudo modprobe -r hid-ntrig
sudo modprobe hid-ntrig
sudo service gdm start
```
There have been times when I have tried to shut down gdm and something gets stuck in X so I end up having to kill the process:
```
sudo service gdm stop
<control-c>
ps -ef|grep X
```
I will then see something like this:
```

root     **25457**  2597  4 08:39 tty7     00:03:38 /usr/bin/X -br -novtswitch -quiet -nolisten tcp :0 vt7 -auth /var/run/xauth/A:0-1YUFyX

```
When you do the the process check (ps -ef) you will see a line similar to the one above.  In order to kill the process, you need the process ID.  In this case it is the item in bold--25457.  To kill that process:
```
sudo kill -9 25457
```
I have found that a normal kill does not stop X.  From there I can reload the hid-ntrig module and restart:
```
sudo modprobe -r hid-ntrig
sudo modprobe hid-ntrig
sudo service gdm start
```
The other option to try is to see if you can shut things down after you kill X:
```
sudo shutdown -h now
```

Now one question that I have is when you restart, what happens?  Does the touch and right-click no longer works?  If that is the case, can you check /var/log/Xorg.0.log to see what driver is being used?  It sounds like another driver is taking over.  Why it only works every once in a while is a little strange.

> Audio works, but plugging in headphones doesn't stop playback on the external speakers. That almost defeats the purpose of headphones.At this point, I don't think that that our sound card has been updated enough yet.  I have found that we are not getting the SPDIF jack to work yet either.  EDIT:  I found that it does work in Lucid so I must have something configured incorrectly in Gentoo.  Right now the driver is close enough for us to be able to use headphones but there is no individual setting for each line-out jack.

> My system lists microphones, but I don't see their activity indicators moving, so more might be needed there. A brief shot at audacity didn't register any sound. The dual mics is a cool feature ... more possibilities in signal processing if I understand correctly.You will need to play around with the volume settings for the digital, line-in, and microphones.  It took me a while before I was able to get the sound to register in the microphone.  After that, I was getting some background noise from the monitor and the fan.

> I'm hoping for some progress on rotation, multi-touch (in addition to learning more about multi-input X), shut-down, and touch-and-hold as right-click.

Right now, the fglrx (the proprietary graphics driver) does not work with the new xorg server that uses the MPX.  Because of this, the radeon driver can be used.  The driver is updated enough to work with the 3D enhancements but the fan control is not as good as the fglrx driver.

I have been able to use the mouse and touch as two separate pointers, but I am still trying to get the two fingers to report at the same time.  I have been playing with the code from ENAC to help get things moving.

### Post by digitalchemystery on 2010-01-15
Things have been working off/on for me, but I think I'm getting a better understanding of the factors involved.

The way to ensure proper functioning:
upon restarting, use the touchpad as the first input device at login screen

Ways to ensure improper functioning:
- Touch the screen as the first thing upon seeing login screen (causes system to freeze)
- Hover the stylus over the screen (causes tracking to be off on both touch and stylus, and clicks won't register from either device)


Can anyone confirm/anti-confirm these behaviors?

Still having the "light touch doesn't trigger both X and Y" and sends cursor to top or left problem.   I'll try they newest wacom driver soon...

on wakeup single touch events don't initialize the touchscreen; it needs a multitouch event before it sends the cursor to the proper position.

I've fixed my "X server dies on sleep after rotate" bug... I found that using the "Gnome Failsafe" session, selectable by allowing timed login eliminates the problem... but then many system services are unavailable.   The last few days have been spend patching things together so those services run anyways, system is "back together" for the most part.  Let me know if anyone needs the gory details...

### Post by bobmuub on 2010-01-23
regarding my post 141, out of curiosity i experimented with my xorg.conf "Device" line, and then i did start experiencing the issue of input locking up if the first input after reboot was the touchscreen... as long as immediately after reboot i use touchpad before touchscreen, all is well.

the xorg.conf change i made was from:
/dev/input/by-path/pci-0000:00:14.5-usb-0:2:1.0-event-mouse
to:
/dev/input/by-path/pci-0000:00:14.5-usb-0:2:1.1-event-mouse

i did this to be in sync with ayuthia's README.txt from the "prebuilt.tar.bz2" file from post 81.  upon making this xorg.conf change, touch was not working at all.  it was after i changed the .1 back to .0 that touch worked again, but with this startup lockup hazard.

not a huge issue, BUT, i was going to try to resolve by rebooting to windows and reinstalling the driver... when windows booted i noticed it updating several drivers automatically.  i'm not certain that touchscreen was included, but there was a couple "unknown input device"s or something like that which were updated.  i decided just to reboot to linux again without explicitly re-installing any drivers manually myself, and it seems the lockup-if-first-input-is-touchscreen problem is gone.  i'm not sure if there is some sort of firmware tickling every time windows boots, or if one of these unknown driver updates fixed something, and i wonder if a simple reboot like this is the "reset" ayuthia describes in post 133, but things are working very well again at this point.

Thanks for removing the logging comments!  I have not had a chance to update it because I have been trying to get the multitouch working in Lucid and see if KDE4.4 really does have multitouch capabilities in Lucid and Gentoo.  I have not had much luck with it though.  I am able to get the two-fingers to report and have their own cursor, but I can't seem to get them to do any of the things that I have seen in the demos.  However I have found another quirk with the firmware that might need a workaround for the multitouch to work well...

> commented out the logging stuff... still "jittery" ... win7 firmware now, software update may have mrade ntrig device more sensive than it was previously, single finger-press can look like a multitouch event?


Actually, the single finger-press looking like a multitouch event is a feature.  It is based on the width of the finger.  I tried to set it up so that a fingertip touches the screen, it will treat it like a single finger, but if you press down a little harder, (it creates a bigger finger print) it is enough to register as a multitouch event.  I did this because I was too lazy to use two-fingers and can scroll using my thumb when I am holding the side of the screen (I am not for sure if that made sense).  If you don't like that option, let me know.  I can always set it up so that it can be turned on/off in xorg.conf/.fdi.

### Post by Ayuthia on 2010-01-30
> **floyd0815 said:**
> Thx for having a look on my doing and helping me out!
The German communities hasn't been helpful to me.

**And many thanks in advance for doing all this.**
:D

**Other question:**
Do I have to edit some .fdi files to prevent hal from loading ntrig?

**xorg.log:**


I've commented out the N-trig section from /usr/share/hal/fdi/policy/20thirdparty/10-linuxwacom.fdi with no changes in the xorg log or in touch behavior.
I read this in step 2 of this post:
[http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1252492](http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1252492)

Should I reedit that file to normal or an other one?

Floyd
I am glad that we are able to help! 

What I have done for mine is add some entries in /etc/hal/fdi/preprobe to ignore certain items like input.tablet for input events so that it will not get assigned to evdev and then added the entries that are for the hardware device itself so that it will get ignored in the .fdi check.  This firmware will assign some things to if0 even though there is nothing there.  Blocking them out will help with using the xsetntrig commands to turn off touch or to rotate.

To simplify things a little, I attached a package that has the .fdi entries needed.  They are found in [post 75]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=8572636&postcount=75").  Hope that helps.

### Post by floyd0815 on 2010-01-31
Thats no problem, I made a shortcut in the dockbarx.

I've also tried to patch the synaptics driver and synclient to rotate the touchpad too when the screen rotates but I'm too f**king dumm for it.
Scrolling was broken and I got some errors when I have tried to rotate it after I made the steps of this how to:
[http://cc.oulu.fi/~rantalai/synaptics/](http://cc.oulu.fi/~rantalai/synaptics/)

If someone else want too try it, **GOOD LUCK AND PLEASE POST THE RESULTS!**

**EDIT:**
**Google Earth can handle multitouch without any patches/plugins?!?**
I can zoom in and out like on the iPhone, only the jittery cursor does sometimes avoid it.
I'm lovin it!

**And does your suspend works normal?**
Because after I've suspended it one time, it doesn't work anymore as long as I don't reboot.
I've found some bug-reports, but in that cases id doesn't work at all.
**EDIT:**Suspend works now fine, I've forgotten to edit the /etc/default/acpi-support.

I also just noticed, that the ir-remote works perfectly, without any patches or something.
That saved a lot of time! -No configuring LIRC-

**Some things I did: (for those who care)**
=> _raise scrollbar width and disable tooltips:_
   Make a file in your home directory "gedit /home/<USER>/.gtkrc-2.0"
   with following in it:
```
style "scroll"
{
    GtkScrollbar::slider-width        = 20
}

class "*" style "scroll"

gtk-enable-tooltips = 0
```

=>_Firefox: scrolling like Acrobat reader_
Install addon "grab and drag"

=>_Are you often draging things out of the menu instead of starting it?_
Install "Gnomenu", and the problems are gone.

I put your "deactivate tooltips" command in .gtkrc-2.0.  It works. I tried it before with my own "fat scrollbars" gtk command and it didn't work. ### Post by Ayuthia on 2010-02-05
> **simartem said:**
> Does the touch-screen function work fast without any problems with Ubuntu installed?
It all depends on who you ask.  The touchscreen does work fast in Linux.  Configuring the device has not been as easy and Lucid is looking like it might be the first one to support these laptops out of the box (except for the newest firmware release).  It is also recommended that you keep Windows on the laptop for firmware updates.

Right now, N-trig does not provide any Linux support so Linux developers are creating the code to make it work.  Each firmware change has required some coding changes.

So if you don't mind reading some guides to install and configure, it will work fine.  You might want to know a little bit of Linux and feel comfortable using Linux before starting on this because it does require the Terminal to make it work.

---

### Post by henry_k on 2010-02-08
**Concerning Ubuntu upgrade from 9.10-17 to 9.10-19**, I was so happy that the screen rotations, stylus and touch worked so well (see my post #87), but the upgrade has thrown a wrench in the works.

The obvious solution is to continue starting up with the 9.10-17 version. What I did to make the starting up a bit more convenient was to edit the /boot/grub/grub.cfg file so as to put the 9.10-17 option ahead of the 9.10-19 option. Of course, one needs to be very careful with editing the file -- in fact, the file starts with a warning not to edit it. Hence, I proceeded very cautiously:

henry@hp:~$ cd /boot/grub
henry@hp:/boot/grub$ sudo cp grub.cfg grub.cfg_
henry@hp:/boot/grub$ sudo chmod u+w grub.cfg_
henry@hp:/boot/grub$ sudo gedit grub.cfg_

Then, just below he line 
### BEGIN /etc/grub.d/10_linux ###
I moved the block with the menuentries for 2.6.31-19-generic and 2.6.31-19-generic (recovery mode) to follow the block with the menuentries for 2.6.31-17-generic and 2.6.31-17-generic (recovery mode).
Then, after making darn sure I made no mistake, I proceeded:

henry@hp:/boot/grub$ sudo cp grub.cfg grub.cfg.bak
henry@hp:/boot/grub$ sudo cp grub.cfg_ grub.cfg
henry@hp:/boot/grub$ chmod u-w grub.cfg

And that was that. Of course, I have just been bypassing the real problem. Maybe our friend Ayuthia has a better, a more fundamental way of making the latest version of Ubuntu 9.10 work. But in the meantime I am happy because things appear to work still very nicely.

Now the waiting is for Lucid Lynx 10.04 LTS. What will it do for our beautiful HP tx2 computers?

H.

---

### Post by dyslexia on 2010-02-15
Some quick notes;

Amazing how a few small changes can make a big difference in the responsiveness of the touch panel.    For example, enabling "xf86WcmFilterCoord" in "usbTabletPC" in "wcmUSB.c" in "linuxwacom-0.5.9" seems to get rid of the "jitter effect" and adds a "cursor slide" feature, which might be nicer if it were a bit faster.

Am using Ayuthia's hid-ntrig.c now ;  rafi's new one is a bit too bleeding edge for me, may switch back later.

64-bit for sure:)

### Post by Ayuthia on 2010-03-03
Ok!  Once you have Ubuntu installed, you might as well update the system to be up-to-date and then once that is complete, please post the result of:
```
uname -a
```so that we can know which kernel revision you are using.  That will allow us to be able to build a kernel module for you and provide you a pre-patched linuxwacom driver.  It will most likely be the easiest way to go.  Please also remind us that you are using Vista and if you have installed the fglrx graphics driver in case other posts come along.

---

### Post by Ayuthia on 2010-03-07
For Karmic, you can first download this package and install the kernel module:
```

cd
mkdir ntrig
cd ntrig
wget http://linuxfans.keryxproject.org/packages/ntrig/ayuthia/karmic/2.6.31-20-generic/hid-ntrig.ko
sudo cp hid-ntrig.ko /lib/modules/2.6.31-20-generic/kernel/drivers/hid/

```
Once that is installed, you can restart.  Then you can check to make sure that it installed:
```
xinput --list
```
You should see your N-trig devices listed as N-Trig Pen and N-Trig Touchscreen.  Please post that result.  If it is successful, we can then go to the next step of compiling and installing the linuxwacom driver.

As for Lucid, I don't recall whether the wacom driver works out of the box or not.  In my directory, I have a patched version so I am guessing that it does not work out of the box with the wacom driver.  However, I was thinking that the evdev driver worked fine with it.

I will most likely be reinstalling Lucid on my laptop again soon and will figure out what needs to be built if anything.

---

### Post by Ayuthia on 2010-03-07
Before we go further, I don't see the touchscreen portion for the N-trig device.  The information that you show only as the stylus.  Can you attach the results of:
```

cd
lshal > lshal.txt

```
It will provide a similar list of devices along with what hal is trying to attach to the device.

---

### Post by Ayuthia on 2010-03-09
Good!  The next step is to install the linuxwacom driver.  Use the following [guide]("http://linuxfans.keryxproject.org/?page_id=97") up to and including the Compile step.  At this point, do not perform the Configure section.  We will work on that together.  We will be most likely using xorg.conf for this.

---

### Post by Austin25 on 2010-03-09
Ok, I got through that. (A few typos, but easy enough)
Now for Xorg.conf right?

---

### Post by Austin25 on 2010-03-12
What do I do now?

---

### Post by Ayuthia on 2010-03-12
We now should try and block out hal from assigning things to the device.  To prepare things, we will need to create a few directories:
```

sudo mkdir /etc/hal/fdi/preprobe
sudo mkdir /etc/hal/fdi/preprobe/10osvendor
sudo mkdir /etc/hal/fdi/preprobe/20thirdparty

```
Then we need to create the following files:
```

gksu gedit /etc/hal/fdi/preprobe/10-x11-input.fdi

```
Add the following:
```

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="ISO-8859-1"?>
<deviceinfo version="0.2">
  <device>
    <match key="info.product" contains="N-trig DuoSense">
    <match key="info.capabilities" contains="input.touchpad">
      <match key="/org/freedesktop/Hal/devices/computer:system.kernel.name"
             string="Linux">
        <merge key="info.ignore" type="bool">true</merge>
      </match>
    </match>
    </match>
  </device>
</deviceinfo>

```
Next, create the following file:
```
gksu gedit /etc/hal/fdi/preprobe/20thirdparty/10-ntrig.fdi
```
and add the following:
```

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?> <!-- -*- SGML -*- -->
<deviceinfo version="0.2">
    <!-- N-Trig Duosense Electromagnetic Digitizer -->
  <device>
    <match key="info.product" contains="N-Trig Pen">
       <merge key="info.ignore" type="bool">true</merge>
    </match>
  </device>
  <device>
    <match key="info.product" contains="N-Trig MultiTouch">
       <merge key="info.ignore" type="bool">true</merge>
    </match>
  </device>
  <device>
    <match key="info.product" contains="N-Trig Touchscreen">
       <merge key="info.ignore" type="bool">true</merge>
    </match>
  </device>
</deviceinfo>

```
Next, create the following:
```

sudo cp /usr/share/hal/fdi/policy/20thirdparty/11-x11-synaptics.fdi /etc/hal/fdi/policy/20thirdparty/

```
and add:
```

<match key="info.product" contains="Synaptics">

```
before or after the first <match line.  Mine looks like this:
```

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="ISO-8859-1"?>
<deviceinfo version="0.2">
  <device>
    <match key="info.product" contains="Synaptics">
    <match key="info.capabilities" contains="input.touchpad">    
        <merge key="input.x11_driver" type="string">synaptics</merge>
        <!-- Arbitrary options can be passed to the driver using
             the input.x11_options property since xorg-server-1.5. -->
        <!-- EXAMPLES:
        Switch on shared memory, enables the driver to be configured at runtime
        -->
<!--    <merge key="input.x11_options.Protocol" type="auto-dev">true</merge> -->
    <merge key="input.x11_options.SHMConfig" type="string">true</merge>
    <merge key="input.x11_options.SendCoreEvents" type="string">true</merge>
    <merge key="input.x11_options.Emulate3Buttons" type="string">true</merge>
    <merge key="input.x11_options.LeftEdge" type="string">1700</merge>
    <merge key="input.x11_options.RightEdge" type="string">5300</merge>
    <merge key="input.x11_options.TopEdge" type="string">1700</merge>
    <merge key="input.x11_options.BottomEdge" type="string">4200</merge>
    <merge key="input.x11_options.VertEdgeScroll" type="string">true</merge>
    <merge key="input.x11_options.VertScrollDelta" type="string">100</merge>

    <merge key="input.x11_options.TapButton1" type="string">1</merge>
    <merge key="input.x11_options.TapButton2" type="string">2</merge>
    <merge key="input.x11_options.TapButton3" type="string">3</merge>

    <!-- If on, circular scrolling is used
    <merge key="input.x11_options.CircularScrolling" type="string">true</merge>
        -->
    <!-- For other possible options, check CONFIGURATION DETAILS in synaptics man page -->
    </match>
    </match>
  </device>
</deviceinfo>

```
After you do this, please post the results of lshal again and also provide a copy of your xorg.conf file.

### Post by Ayuthia on 2010-03-13
I think that your xorg.conf should look like this:
```

Section "Screen"
    Identifier    "Default Screen"
    DefaultDepth    24
EndSection

Section "Module"
    Load    "glx"
EndSection

Section "Device"
    Identifier    "Default Device"
    Driver    "fglrx"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
    Identifier    "stylus" 
    Driver        "wacom" 
    Option        "Device"    "/dev/input/event7"
    Option        "Type"        "stylus"
    Option        "USB"         "on" 
    Option        "Button2"     "3"  # make side-switch a right button 
    Option        "TopX"        "0"
    Option        "TopY"        "0"
    Option        "BottomX"    "9600"
    Option        "BottomY"    "7200"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
    Identifier    "touch" 
    Driver        "wacom" 
    Option        "Device"    "/dev/input/event8"
    Option        "Type"           "touch"
    Option        "USB"            "on" 
    Option        "TopX"        "0"
    Option        "TopY"        "0"
    Option        "BottomX"    "9600"
    Option        "BottomY"    "7200"
EndSection

Section "ServerLayout"
    Identifier    "Default Layout"
    Screen        "Default Screen"
    InputDevice    "stylus"    "SendCoreEvents"
    InputDevice    "touch"        "SendCoreEvents"
EndSection

```
If your touch does not work, try event10.  If your pen does not work, try event9.  It has been a while since I worked with Vista's firmware and I tend to get the events mixed up.

### Post by Ayuthia on 2010-03-13
This [link]("https://help.ubuntu.com/community/Wacom") is probably the best place to see how to do it because it comes with pictures.  Basically in applications like GIMP, there is an Input Devices section where you configure the pen so that it can handle pressure.

Let me know if you run into problems with that information.

### Post by mcoleman44 on 2010-03-19
This is something Ayuthia had to help me with a few weeks ago. Here is what he told me to do, and it worked.

```
sudo mkdir /etc/hal/fdi/preprobe/10osvendor
gksu gedit /etc/hal/fdi/preprobe/10osvendor/10-x11-input.fdi
```

and add the following:
```
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="ISO-8859-1"?>
<deviceinfo version="0.2">
  <device>
    <match key="info.capabilities" contains="input.tablet">
      <match key="/org/freedesktop/Hal/devices/computer:system.kernel.name"
             string="Linux">
    <merge key="info.ignore" type="bool">true</merge>
      </match>
    </match>
  </device>
</deviceinfo>
```

and save it.  This will tell hal to ignore the input.tablet rule that assigns evdev to any tablet.

The next file:
```
gksu gedit /etc/hal/fdi/policy/20thirdparty/11-x11-synaptics.fdi
```

and add:
```
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="ISO-8859-1"?>
<deviceinfo version="0.2">
  <device>
    <match key="info.capabilities" contains="input.touchpad">
    <match key="info.product" contains="Synaptics">
        <merge key="input.x11_driver" type="string">synaptics</merge>
        <!-- Arbitrary options can be passed to the driver using
             the input.x11_options property since xorg-server-1.5. -->
        <!-- EXAMPLES:
        Switch on shared memory, enables the driver to be configured at runtime
    <merge key="input.x11_options.SHMConfig" type="string">true</merge>

    Maximum movement of the finger for detecting a tap
    <merge key="input.x11_options.MaxTapMove" type="string">2000</merge>

    Enable vertical scrolling when dragging along the right edge
    <merge key="input.x11_options.VertEdgeScroll" type="string">true</merge>

    Enable vertical scrolling when dragging with two fingers anywhere on the touchpad
    <merge key="input.x11_options.VertTwoFingerScroll" type="string">true</merge>

    Enable horizontal scrolling when dragging with two fingers anywhere on the touchpad
    <merge key="input.x11_options.HorizTwoFingerScroll" type="string">true</merge>

    If on, circular scrolling is used
    <merge key="input.x11_options.CircularScrolling" type="string">true</merge>

    For other possible options, check CONFIGURATION DETAILS in synaptics man page
        -->
    </match>
    </match>
  </device>
</deviceinfo>
```

Restart and it should work. All credit goes to Ayuthia.

Yeah, you need to add wacom to that list.

### Post by Austin25 on 2010-03-19
so like this?
```
# /etc/modules: kernel modules to load at boot time.
#
# This file contains the names of kernel modules that should be loaded
# at boot time, one per line. Lines beginning with "#" are ignored.

lp
rtc
uinput
hp-wmi
wacom
```

<deviceinfo version="0.2">
  <device>
    <match key="info.capabilities" contains="input.touchpad">
        <merge key="input.x11_driver" type="string">synaptics</merge>
        <!-- Arbitrary options can be passed to the driver using
             the input.x11_options property since xorg-server-1.5. -->
        <!-- EXAMPLES:
        Switch on shared memory, enables the driver to be configured at runtime
    <merge key="input.x11_options.SHMConfig" type="string">true</merge>

    Maximum movement of the finger for detecting a tap
    <merge key="input.x11_options.MaxTapMove" type="string">2000</merge>

    Enable vertical scrolling when dragging along the right edge
    <merge key="input.x11_options.VertEdgeScroll" type="string">true</merge>

    Enable vertical scrolling when dragging with two fingers anywhere on the touchpad
    <merge key="input.x11_options.VertTwoFingerScroll" type="string">true</merge>

    Enable horizontal scrolling when dragging with two fingers anywhere on the touchpad
    <merge key="input.x11_options.HorizTwoFingerScroll" type="string">true</merge>

    If on, circular scrolling is used
    <merge key="input.x11_options.CircularScrolling" type="string">true</merge>

    For other possible options, check CONFIGURATION DETAILS in synaptics man page
        -->
        <match key="/org/freedesktop/Hal/devices/computer:system.hardware.product" string="Inspiron 1011">
            <merge key="input.x11_options.JumpyCursorThreshold" type="string">90</merge>
            <merge key="input.x11_options.AreaBottomEdge" type="string">4100</merge>

<deviceinfo version="0.2">
  <device>

    <!-- KVM emulates a USB graphics tablet which works in absolute coordinate mode -->
    <match key="input.product" contains="QEMU USB Tablet">
       <merge key="input.x11_driver" type="string">evdev</merge>
    </match>

    <!-- FIXME: Support tablets too. -->
    <match key="info.capabilities" contains="input.mouse">
      <merge key="input.x11_driver" type="string">mouse</merge>
      <match key="/org/freedesktop/Hal/devices/computer:system.kernel.name"
             string="Linux">
        <merge key="input.x11_driver" type="string">evdev</merge>
      </match>
    </match>

    <match key="info.capabilities" contains="input.tablet">
      <match key="/org/freedesktop/Hal/devices/computer:system.kernel.name"
             string="Linux">
        <merge key="input.x11_driver" type="string">evdev</merge>
      </match>
    </match>

    <match key="info.capabilities" contains="input.keys">
      <!-- If we're using Linux, we use evdev by default (falling back to
           keyboard otherwise). -->
      <merge key="input.x11_driver" type="string">keyboard</merge>
      <match key="/org/freedesktop/Hal/devices/computer:system.kernel.name"
             string="Linux">
        <merge key="input.x11_driver" type="string">evdev</merge>
      </match>
    </match>
  </device>
</deviceinfo>

        <match key="@info.parent:pnp.id" contains_outof="WACf008;WACf009;WACf00A;WACf00B;WACf00C;WACf00D;WACf00E;WACf010;FUJ02e7;FUJ02e9">

        <match key="info.product" contains="Tablet">

      <append key="info.callouts.add" type="strlist">hal-setup-wacom</append>

      <append key="wacom.types" type="strlist">pad</append>
        </match>
      </match>
    </match>
  </device>
</deviceinfo>

### Post by mcoleman44 on 2010-03-20
Your /etc/hal/fdi/preprobe/20thirdparty.10-wacom.fdi should look like this:
```
<!-- -*- SGML -*- -->
&#8722;
<deviceinfo version="0.2">
<!-- N-Trig Duosense Electromagnetic Digitizer -->
&#8722;
<device>
&#8722;
<match key="info.product" contains="N-Trig Pen">
&#8722;
<match key="info.parent" contains="if0">
<merge key="info.ignore" type="string">true</merge>
</match>
</match>
</device>
&#8722;
<device>
&#8722;
<match key="info.product" contains="N-Trig Touchscreen">
&#8722;
<match key="info.parent" contains="if0">
<merge key="info.ignore" type="string">true</merge>
</match>
</match>
</device>
&#8722;
<device>
&#8722;
<match key="info.product" contains="N-Trig Pen">
&#8722;
<match key="info.parent" contains="if1">
<merge key="info.ignore" type="string">true</merge>
</match>
</match>
</device>
&#8722;
<device>
&#8722;
<match key="info.product" contains="N-Trig Touchscreen">
&#8722;
<match key="info.parent" contains="if1">
<merge key="info.ignore" type="string">true</merge>
</match>
</match>
</device>
</deviceinfo>
```

### Post by mcoleman44 on 2010-03-20
[FONT=monospace]gksudo gedit /etc/udev/rules.d/90-n-trig.rules
[/FONT]```
# udev rules for n-trig tablets.
#
# These rules were compiled for the Ubuntu/Debian GNU/Linux distribution, but others may,
# and indeed are encouraged to, use them also.
#
# Should you do so, PLEASE CO-ORDINATE ANY CHANGES OR ADDITIONS of new devices with ????
# so that we can try to present users with a standard set of device nodes which they can
# rely on across the board.

KERNEL!="event[0-9]*", GOTO="n-trig_end"

# To mirror linuxwacom, we keep the input/n-trig link as the generic stylus device.
ATTRS{name}=="N-Trig Touchscreen", SUBSYSTEM=="input", ENV{x11_driver}="wacom", SYMLINK+="input/n-trig-touch"
ATTRS{name}=="N-Trig MultiTouch", SUBSYSTEM=="input", ENV{x11_driver}="wacom", SYMLINK+="input/n-trig-mttouch"
ATTRS{name}=="N-Trig Pen", SUBSYSTEM=="input", ENV{x11_driver}="wacom", SYMLINK+="input/n-trig"

# Check and repossess the device if a module other than the n-trig one is already bound
# to it.
ATTRS{idVendor}=="1b96", ACTION=="add", RUN+="check_driver ntrig $devpath $env{ID_BUS}"

LABEL="n-trig_end"
```

gksuod gedit /lib/udev/check_driver
```
#!/bin/sh
#

#logger check_driver called with: $1 - $2 - $3 -

wanted=$1
devpath=$2
bustype=$3

device=$(readlink /sys/$devpath/device)
device=${device##*/}
driver=$(readlink /sys/$devpath/device/driver)
driver=${driver##*/}

logger device $device is bound to the $driver driver

if [ "$driver" != "$wanted" ]; then
    logger must rebind
    echo -n "$device" > /sys/$devpath/device/driver/unbind
    echo -n "$device" > /sys/bus/$bustype/drivers/$wanted/bind
else
    logger no need to rebind
fi
```

sudo chmod +x /lib/udev/check_driver

### Post by Ayuthia on 2010-03-20
When it does not connect, can you post the results of:
```
ls -l /dev/input/*
```
From your Xorg.0.log it looks like it is not reading anything from /dev/input/event7 and event8.  I am thinking that the location shifted.  If that is the case, I will see if I can update the udev rules so that it will always point to the if0 spot for you so that you can use /dev/input/n-trig for the stylus and /dev/input/n-trig-touch for the touch instead of using the event numbers.

---

oh I see it! I need to set my xorg.confto event 11 and 12 right?
It might actually be event9 and even10.  The n-trig pointers are linking to the if1 portion instead of the if0.  You can confirm this in your lshal results.

### Post by Austin25 on 2010-03-20
I did backup my harddrive just after mcoleman44 told me to put "wacom in /etc/modules". Should I restore it? It would probably take a couple of hours and could be dangerous.

---

### Post by Ayuthia on 2010-03-20
> **Austin25 said:**
> I did backup my harddrive just after mcoleman44 told me to put "wacom in /etc/modules". Should I restore it? It would probably take a couple of hours and could be dangerous.

There is no real need to restore.  You can remove the wacom from /etc/modules if you want.  That module is not really used for our device because we are using the hid-ntrig kernel module.  If you leave it there, it won't hurt anything.

---

### Post by Austin25 on 2010-03-20

Btw, thank you for continuing the war against my stupidity and overly complex hardware.

---

### Post by Ayuthia on 2010-03-20
> **Austin25 said:**
> No, he told me to add many things afterward. see everything after [this point.]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=1304273&page=22")
Btw, thank you for continuing the war against my stupidity and overly complex hardware.
All of the steps that have been provided so far has been fine.  I am just reviewing your udev information and checking to see if there is a way to revise the udev rules so that it grabs the correct event.

Once we get this figured out a little better, I am going to try to figure out a way to create an installer and configuration process so that we don't have to go through all of these steps.  The hard part is the fact that N-trig has changed their firmware reporting on almost every firmware release so it has made it more challenging to create a good set of rules to configure it.

---

### Post by Austin25 on 2010-03-20
OK, Ayuthia, I will test it if you make it, also I would suggest creating a new thread for it. Another idea would be to make 2 versions of the installer: one for Vista firmware and one for W7 firmware. The other idea would be to have that as an option in the installer, and have different versions for different kernel versions.

---

### Post by Ayuthia on 2010-03-20
Let's try the following replace /etc/udev/rules.d/90-n-trig.rules with the following:
```

# udev rules for n-trig tablets.
#
# These rules were compiled for the Ubuntu/Debian GNU/Linux distribution, but others may,
# and indeed are encouraged to, use them also.
#
# Should you do so, PLEASE CO-ORDINATE ANY CHANGES OR ADDITIONS of new devices with ????
# so that we can try to present users with a standard set of device nodes which they can
# rely on across the board.

KERNEL!="event[0-9]*", GOTO="n-trig_end"

# To mirror linuxwacom, we keep the input/n-trig link as the generic stylus device.
ATTRS{name}=="N-Trig Touchscreen", ATTRS{phys}=="usb-0000:00:14.5-2/input0", SUBSYSTEM=="input", SYMLINK+="input/hp-n-trig-touch"
ATTRS{name}=="N-Trig Touchscreen", SUBSYSTEM=="input", SYMLINK+="input/n-trig-touch"
ATTRS{name}=="N-Trig MultiTouch", SUBSYSTEM=="input", SYMLINK+="input/n-trig-mttouch"
ATTRS{name}=="N-Trig Pen", SUBSYSTEM=="input", SYMLINK+="input/n-trig"
ATTRS{name}=="N-Trig Pen", ATTRS{phys}=="usb-0000:00:14.5-2/input0", SUBSYSTEM=="input", SYMLINK+="input/hp-n-trig"

# Check and repossess the device if a module other than the n-trig/linuxwacom one is
# already bound to it.
ATTRS{idVendor}=="1b96", ACTION=="add", RUN+="check_driver wacom $devpath $env{ID_BUS}"

LABEL="n-trig_end"

```

Then update your xorg.conf to point to /dev/input/hp-n-trig-touch and /dev/input/hp-n-trig.

If this works, it will be a temporary solution for HP laptops not using the newer Win 7 firmware.  I am going to have to research this further to see if there is a better solution so that we don't have various versions built into the rules.

---

xsetwacom list
lsmod
xinput --list

I was looking to see if there were any errors(EE)

```xorg.conf
```
Section "Screen"
    Identifier    "Default Screen"
    DefaultDepth    24
EndSection

Section "Module"
    Load    "glx"
EndSection

Section "Device"
    Identifier    "Default Device"
    Driver    "fglrx"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice" 
    Identifier        "stylus" 
    Driver            "wacom" 
    Option        "Device"    "/dev/input/by-path/pci-0000:00:14.5-usb-0:2:1.0-event-mouse"
    Option            "Type"        "stylus"
    Option            "USB"         "on" 
    Option            "Button2"     "3"  # make side-switch a right button 
    Option        "TopX"        "0"
    Option        "TopY"        "0"
    Option        "BottomX"    "9600"
    Option        "BottomY"    "7200"
EndSection 

#Section "InputDevice" 
#      Identifier        "eraser" 
#      Driver            "wacom" 
#    Option        "Device"    "/dev/input/wacom"
#      Option            "Type"           "eraser"
#      Option            "USB"            "on" 
#EndSection 

Section "InputDevice" 
    Identifier        "touch" 
    Driver            "wacom" 
    Option        "Device"    "/dev/input/by-path/pci-0000:00:14.5-usb-0:2:1.0-event-mouse"
    Option          "Type"           "touch"
    Option          "USB"            "on" 
    Option        "TopX"        "0"
    Option        "TopY"        "0"
    Option        "BottomX"    "9600"
    Option        "BottomY"    "7200"
EndSection

Section "ServerLayout"
    Identifier    "Default Layout"
    Screen        "Default Screen"
    InputDevice    "stylus"    "SendCoreEvents"
#   Remove the comment below if you have an eraser.
#    InputDevice    "eraser"    "SendCoreEvents"
    InputDevice    "touch"        "SendCoreEvents"
EndSection
```

		> Are you using 90-hid-ntrig.rules? I don't think so. They don't ring any bells... What are they?

Thanks for looking at this for me.

---

### Post by mcoleman44 on 2010-04-05
gksudo gedit /etc/udev/rules.d/90-n-trig.rules

```
# udev rules for n-trig tablets.
#
# These rules were compiled for the Ubuntu/Debian GNU/Linux distribution, but others may,
# and indeed are encouraged to, use them also.
#
# Should you do so, PLEASE CO-ORDINATE ANY CHANGES OR ADDITIONS of new devices with ????
# so that we can try to present users with a standard set of device nodes which they can
# rely on across the board.

KERNEL!="event[0-9]*", GOTO="n-trig_end"

# To mirror linuxwacom, we keep the input/n-trig link as the generic stylus device.
ATTRS{name}=="N-Trig Touchscreen", SUBSYSTEM=="input", ENV{x11_driver}="wacom", SYMLINK+="input/n-trig-touch"
ATTRS{name}=="N-Trig MultiTouch", SUBSYSTEM=="input", ENV{x11_driver}="wacom", SYMLINK+="input/n-trig-mttouch"
ATTRS{name}=="N-Trig Pen", SUBSYSTEM=="input", ENV{x11_driver}="wacom", SYMLINK+="input/n-trig"

# Check and repossess the device if a module other than the n-trig one is already bound
# to it.
ATTRS{idVendor}=="1b96", ACTION=="add", RUN+="check_driver ntrig $devpath $env{ID_BUS}"

LABEL="n-trig_end"
```gksudo gedit /lib/udev/check_driver

```
#!/bin/sh
#

#logger check_driver called with: $1 - $2 - $3 -

wanted=$1
devpath=$2
bustype=$3

device=$(readlink /sys/$devpath/device)
device=${device##*/}
driver=$(readlink /sys/$devpath/device/driver)
driver=${driver##*/}

logger device $device is bound to the $driver driver

if [ "$driver" != "$wanted" ]; then
    logger must rebind
    echo -n "$device" > /sys/$devpath/device/driver/unbind
    echo -n "$device" > /sys/bus/$bustype/drivers/$wanted/bind
else
    logger no need to rebind
fi
```save and then:
sudo chmod +x /lib/udev/check_driver

In your xorg change touch section to /dev/input/n-trig-touch
and stylus to /dev/input/n-trig

```
stylus     stylus

---

### Post by mcoleman44 on 2010-04-05
Try using /dev/input/event8
for stylus.

If that doesnt work try event7

Also, see if this produces any results when touching the screen with your finger and stylus:
sudo xxd /dev/hidraw1
if that doesnt do anything try it with hidraw0 and hidraw2

---

### Post by lps1 on 2010-04-05
I get inconsistent results changing to events 7 or 8.

With stylus as event 8 and;
- reboot 1: both work
- reboot 2: stylus only
- reboot 3: neither work
- reboot 4: stylus only
- reboot 5: both work
- reboot 6: both work
- reboot 7: neither work
- reboot 8: stylus only

with event 7:
- reboot 1: touch only
- reboot 2: touch only
- reboot 3: neither work
- reboot 4: touch only
- reboot 5: stylus only

I'm baffled -- there doesn't seem to be a pattern...

with stylus set as event 7:
hidraw1 doesn't pick up anything
hidraw0 picks up both touch and pen
hidraw2 doesn't run.

same with event8.

---

### Post by mcoleman44 on 2010-04-05
Ok, were getting ready to add a bunch of files. No need to get scared though. They will either work or not work, they wont mess up your system.
xorg.conf
```
Section "Screen"
    Identifier    "Default Screen"
    DefaultDepth    24
EndSection

Section "Module"
    Load    "glx"
EndSection

Section "Device"
    Identifier    "Default Device"
    Driver    "fglrx"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
    Identifier    "stylus"
    Driver        "wacom"
    Option        "Device"    "/dev/input/event7"
    Option        "Type"        "stylus"
    Option        "USB"        "on"
    Option        "Button2"    "3"    # make stylus button R mouse click
    Option        "TopX"        "0"
    Option        "TopY"        "0"
    Option        "BottomX"    "9600"
    Option        "BottomY"    "7200"
EndSection

#   Remove the comments below if you have an eraser.  Dell XT & XT2?
#Section "InputDevice"
#    Identifier    "eraser"
#    Driver        "wacom"
#    Option        "Device"    "/dev/input/n-trig"
#    Option        "Type"        "eraser"
#    Option        "USB"        "on"
#EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
    Identifier    "touch"
    Driver        "wacom"
    Option        "Device"    "/dev/input/event8"
    Option        "Type"        "touch"
    Option        "USB"        "on"
    Option        "TopX"        "0"
    Option        "TopY"        "0"
    Option        "BottomX"    "9600"
    Option        "BottomY"    "7200"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
    Identifier    "mttouch"
    Driver        "wacom"
    Option        "Device"    "/dev/input/n-trig-mttouch"
    Option        "Type"        "touch"
    Option        "USB"        "on"
    Option        "TopX"        "0"
    Option        "TopY"        "0"
    Option        "BottomX"    "9600"
    Option        "BottomY"    "7200"
EndSection

Section "Device"
    Identifier    "Configured Video Device"
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
    Identifier    "Configured Monitor"
EndSection

Section "Screen"
    Identifier    "Default Screen"
    Monitor        "Configured Monitor"
    Device        "Configured Video Device"
EndSection

Section "ServerLayout"
    Identifier    "X.org Configured"
#    Identifier    "Default Layout"
#    Screen        "Default Screen"
    InputDevice    "stylus"    "SendCoreEvents"
#   Remove the comment below if you have an eraser.
#    InputDevice    "eraser"    "SendCoreEvents"
    InputDevice    "touch"        "SendCoreEvents"
    InputDevice    "mttouch"    "SendCoreEvents"
EndSection

#   Developed with Ayuthia (using Rafi Rubin's Wacom sections as a starting point).
```gksudo gedit /usr/share/hal/fdi/policy/20thirdparty/10-linuxwacom.fdi

```
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?> <!-- -*- SGML -*- -->

<deviceinfo version="0.2">
  <!-- N-Trig Duosense Electromagnetic Digitizer -->
  <device>
    <match key="info.product" contains="N-Trig Pen">
    <match key="info.parent" contains="if0">
       <merge key="info.ignore" type="string">true</merge>
    </match>
    </match>
  </device>
  <device>
    <match key="info.product" contains="N-Trig Touchscreen">
    <match key="info.parent" contains="if0">
        <merge key="info.ignore" type="string">true</merge>
    </match>
    </match>
  </device>
  <device>
    <match key="info.product" contains="N-Trig Pen">
    <match key="info.parent" contains="if1">
       <merge key="info.ignore" type="string">true</merge>
    </match>
    </match>
  </device>
  <device>
    <match key="info.product" contains="N-Trig Touchscreen">
    <match key="info.parent" contains="if1">
        <merge key="info.ignore" type="string">true</merge>
    </match>
    </match>
  </device>
</deviceinfo>
```
Place the file above in to the file below too please.
```
cd /etc/hal/fdi/
sudo mkdir preprobe
sudo mkdir preprobe/20thirdparty
gksudo gedit /etc/hal/fdi/preprobe/20thirdparty/10-linuxwacom.fdi

```Reboot, cross your fingers and hope it works!

### Post by lps1 on 2010-04-05
Thanks.  I tried all that.  What was that trying to do?

On reboot touch didn't work and while the pen did, the cursor was a long way from the pen.  Restarted and neither the pen nor touch work.  Can I return to the previous settings just by going back to the previous xorg.conf?  At least with that one, I was sometimes getting both to work.

---

### Post by mcoleman44 on 2010-04-05
Yeah you can return to the old xorg. I was trying to block hal from grabbing the stylus and touch. Before you change back can you post your xorg0 log. And then after you swith back post your xorg log again. The sudo xxd /dev/hidraw0-2 was to see if the ntrig module was even loading, working and picking up the stylus and finger. Your results show that it is. So now we have to make your xorg.conf to use it correctly.

```Now I'll change back xorg.conf, reboot and paste the log again.

right changed xorg.conf to:
```
Section "Screen"
    Identifier    "Default Screen"
    DefaultDepth    24
EndSection

Section "Module"
    Load    "glx"
EndSection

Section "Device"
    Identifier    "Default Device"
    Driver    "fglrx"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice" 
    Identifier        "stylus" 
    Driver            "wacom" 
    Option        "Device"    "/dev/input/event7"
    Option            "Type"        "stylus"
    Option            "USB"         "on" 
    Option            "Button2"     "3"  # make side-switch a right button 
    Option        "TopX"        "0"
    Option        "TopY"        "0"
    Option        "BottomX"    "9600"
    Option        "BottomY"    "7200"
EndSection 

#Section "InputDevice" 
#      Identifier        "eraser" 
#      Driver            "wacom" 
#    Option        "Device"    "/dev/input/wacom"
#      Option            "Type"           "eraser"
#      Option            "USB"            "on" 
#EndSection 

Section "InputDevice" 
    Identifier        "touch" 
    Driver            "wacom" 
    Option        "Device"    "/dev/input/n-trig-touch"
    Option          "Type"           "touch"
    Option          "USB"            "on" 
    Option        "TopX"        "0"
    Option        "TopY"        "0"
    Option        "BottomX"    "9600"
    Option        "BottomY"    "7200"
EndSection

Section "ServerLayout"
    Identifier    "Default Layout"
    Screen        "Default Screen"
    InputDevice    "stylus"    "SendCoreEvents"
#   Remove the comment below if you have an eraser.
#    InputDevice    "eraser"    "SendCoreEvents"
    InputDevice    "touch"        "SendCoreEvents"
EndSection
```
and rebooting with this (pen worked, touch not) this is the log:

### Post by dyslexia on 2010-04-13

```

```

Actually, HAL is far more capable of assigning devices and running the mission, but for some reason we decided that xorg.conf has to do it, thus HAL has to be told "hands off", use [this post]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=8958787&postcount=195") to configure HAL.

---

### Post by Austin25 on 2010-06-04
It works by default under lucid hold two things:
Capacitive touchscreen won't click.
[LEFT]Side button on the pen left clicks instead of right click.

Is there a quick way to fix this with Xorg.conf or some configuration app?
[/LEFT]

---

### Post by Ayuthia on 2010-06-05
> **Austin25 said:**
> It works by default under lucid hold two things:
Capacitive touchscreen won't click.
[LEFT]Side button on the pen left clicks instead of right click.

Is there a quick way to fix this with Xorg.conf or some configuration app?
[/LEFT]

It should only need a kernel module update.  The current kernel module is missing the necessary event calls to make the click.  You can grab the kernel module source from this [post]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=9236052&postcount=977") or you can try using the [installer]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=9111274&postcount=891") to install it for you.  You will only need to install the ntrig version (instead of using the all option).

---

### Post by Austin25 on 2010-06-06
What do I need where for the installer to work?

---

### Post by Ayuthia on 2010-06-06
> **Austin25 said:**
> What do I need where for the installer to work?

You should just be able to run the application in your home directory and it should run.  It is running a script that will download the packages needed to build the kernel module and then it will compile the module and install it for you.

---

### Post by Ayuthia on 2010-06-23
I thought the I replied yesterday, but I must have either forgotten to submit it or else it somehow disappeared.  Can you try:
```

cd
./installer.py ntrig

```
and see if it makes a difference?

### Post by Austin25 on 2010-06-23
There we go, it's doing something, I'll restart when it's done.

---

### Post by Austin25 on 2010-06-23
OK, so the touch clicks, but the side button on the pen doesn't right click. Do I need to do something with Xorg.conf?

---

### Post by Ayuthia on 2010-06-23
> **Austin25 said:**
> OK, so the touch clicks, but the side button on the pen doesn't right click. Do I need to do something with Xorg.conf?

I think that Lucid might be defaulting the pen to evdev, but I could be wrong.  You can create the following /etc/X11/xorg.conf/10-wacom.conf entry:
from this [link]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=9175064&postcount=945").

---

### Post by Austin25 on 2010-06-23
Perfect. It Worked!
I realize now that there was a bug with how it was before: once you click, you would have to pull the pen away for it to hover below the pen. This fixed it.

---
